title: No purple-team close-out described, despite Threat Scenario format requiring blue team in the room
evidence_quote_or_observation: The document defines three phases — Planning, Execution and Report — and never mentions the blue team, SOC, defenders, joint walk-through, replay or any purple-team close-out activity. The Planning section describes 'a brainstorming session among the operators' (red team only) and the Report section is a written deliverable, not a joint review.
expected: An explicit purple-team close-out phase where red and blue jointly walk through each scenario, discuss observed detections and controls, and agree defensive improvements — a mandatory step for all three Euronext engagement types and the defining feature of a Threat Scenario.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Post-Exploitation / Reporting (joint debrief) (PTES treats the debrief as a deliverable; Euronext's threat-scenario format goes further and requires a live blue+red walk-through.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure phase — replay and 360° feedback (Cited as analogous practice; not invoking TLPT applicability here.)
recommendation_text: Add an explicit 'Purple-team close-out' phase between Execution and Report. Specify required attendees (red team operators, SOC/blue team representative, target system owner), the agenda (per-scenario walk-through, detections observed vs missed, control recommendations) and the output (jointly-signed action list feeding the Report).
owner_hint: Red team lead with Purple team / SOC

title: No authorization / Rules-of-Engagement section: no legal sign-off, no kill-switch, no abort criteria
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the document has no Authorization, RoE, Legal, Sign-off, Kill-switch, Abort or Safe-word section. The closest control is the Scope text 'Clearly define the scope and limitations of the engagement … include any restrictions on techniques, tools, or attack vectors', which describes scope, not an authorization chain.
expected: A named authorization section identifying who signs off (system owner, CISO/Head of Security, Legal), the abort/kill-switch criteria, who can invoke them, the safe-word(s), and the data-handling rules for sensitive content encountered during scenario execution.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions (Rules of Engagement, authorization); NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 — Planning and Rules of Engagement; OSSTMM:OSSTMM Rules of Engagement (Cited from memory.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — Control Team and White Team roles (Cited from memory.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26/27 (general) and RTS on TLPT — control team / test mandate (Cited from memory; verify against live RTS on TLPT.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26/27 (general) and RTS on TLPT — data protection during testing (Cited from memory; verify against live RTS on TLPT.); OTHER:GDPR — legal basis and minimisation for security testing (Cited from memory; verify with Legal/DPO.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Rules of Engagement / Authorization' section to the Planning phase. As a minimum: named approver(s) per scope (system owner + Head of Red Team / CISO), explicit Legal sign-off where personal or production data is in scope, kill-switch and abort criteria, safe-word, and the contacts on call to invoke them.

title: No purple-teaming close-out with the blue team
evidence_quote_or_observation: The final stage for the Operation is to report the outcome of the scenario. This report should contain enough information for the BT to understand every step of the attack, and the reasoning behind them. ... In the report outcomes, it should also contain the Opportunities For Improvements for both the Blue Team and the Red Team, highlighting what went well and what went wrong as well as recommendations on how to improve.
expected: A mandatory joint replay / purple-team workshop with the Blue Team after every Operation, with documented attendees, agenda, detection-vs-action mapping, and agreed defensive improvements.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure phase (replay & 360 feedback) (TIBER-EU mandates a replay workshop in Closure; Euronext applies the same mandatory purple-team close-out to Operations.); PTES:PTES Reporting / Post-Engagement
recommendation_text: Add an explicit 'Purple-team close-out' subsection under Closure: a mandatory live workshop with the BT to replay the scenario, map BT detections (or absence) against each TTP, and capture defensive improvements. Make the workshop a gating step before the Operation can be marked complete.

title: Authorization phase is implicit; no legal sign-off, RoE artefact, safe-words, or abort criteria defined
evidence_quote_or_observation: Complies with all laws, regulations, policies, programs and Rules of Engagement (RoE).
expected: An Authorization phase describing: who signs the engagement letter (legal, risk, business owner), where the RoE document lives, safe-words for abort, kill-switch authority, blue-team notification rules, and pre-flight check before execution.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 Planning / Rules of Engagement; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Preparation / engagement letter
recommendation_text: Add an explicit Authorization section before Research & Preparation listing: the engagement letter / RoE owner, required signatories (legal, risk, business owner), safe-word convention, kill-switch authority, abort criteria, and a pre-flight checklist that must be signed before any RT activity.

title: No mandatory purple-team close-out / replay with the blue team
evidence_quote_or_observation: The Closure section describes only outcome reporting: 'The final stage for the Exercise is to report the outcome of each scenario... In this report, there should also be information regarding the vulnerabilities found, similar to a regular Pentesting report'. There is no replay, walkthrough, or 360-degree session with the BT to translate red findings into blue improvements.
expected: A defined purple-team close-out workshop with the blue team that walks through TTPs used, detections triggered/missed, and agrees defensive improvements; mandatory for every Exercise.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure phase (Replay & 360 feedback) (Closure phase mandates joint replay and 360 feedback even for non-TLPT exercises this is the de facto good practice.); PTES:PTES Reporting / Post-Engagement; PTES:PTES Post-Engagement; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 Section 8 (Post-Testing Activities) (Recommends translating findings into actionable defensive improvements.); PTES:PTES Post-Engagement (Remediation); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure (Remediation Plan); PTES:PTES Post-Exploitation / Cleanup; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 Section 8.3 (Post-Test); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure phase (replay, 360 feedback, remediation plan, attestation) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT (closure artefacts) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation and RTS text.)
recommendation_text: Add a mandatory 'Purple-team replay' step in Closure: a working session with the BT walking the kill chain step-by-step, mapping each TTP to detections/preventions in place, and agreeing concrete defensive improvements. Capture as a deliverable (report annex or separate replay note).
owner_hint: Red team lead + Blue team lead

title: Authorization chain incomplete: no named legal sign-off, no kill-switch / abort criteria, no safe-words
evidence_quote_or_observation: The doc references RoE and that the WT 'is mandated to start, stop and approve attacks', and notes data-handling halts, but never names: (a) the legal/risk sign-off authority required before launch, (b) kill-switch / abort criteria (what conditions stop the exercise), (c) safe-words or out-of-band channels for emergency stop.
expected: Authorization section must name the legal sign-off owner, list explicit abort criteria (production impact, real incident, business event), and define a safe-word / emergency stop channel.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions (Authorization, Emergency Contacts); NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 Section 4 (Planning and Rules of Engagement)
recommendation_text: Add an 'Authorization & Kill-switch' subsection: (1) named legal/risk sign-off prerequisite, (2) explicit abort criteria, (3) safe-word + 24/7 contact channel between RT lead and WT lead, (4) what 'stop' means operationally.

title: Authorization, Rules of Engagement, legal sign-off and kill-switch / abort criteria are not described
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the methodology references 'WT Point of Contact' once (Data Handling) but provides no Authorization section, no Rules of Engagement template, no legal sign-off pathway, no safe-words, no abort/kill-switch criteria, and no description of who approves an engagement.
expected: A methodology must define the authorization chain (sign-off authorities), the Rules of Engagement contents, safe-words, abort/kill-switch conditions, and what triggers an engagement halt — even by reference to a per-engagement RoE template.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions / Rules of Engagement; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 Planning; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Preparation — Engagement Letter / Scoping
recommendation_text: Add a dedicated 'Authorization & Rules of Engagement' section that points to a mandatory RoE template covering: approving authority, legal/risk sign-off, safe-words, kill-switch, abort criteria, blue-team notification rules per engagement type, and indemnities. Make sign-off a hard pre-execution gate.

title: TIBER-EU Preparation elements absent: GTL, scoping doc, engagement letter, critical-functions ID, provider procurement
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — no mention of Generic Threat Landscape (GTL), no scoping document, no engagement letter, no identification of critical/important functions, no procurement criteria for TI/RT providers (the doc does not mention external TI or RT providers at all).
expected: If Exercises are intended to be TIBER-EU / TLPT, the methodology must reference Preparation-phase artefacts: GTL ingestion, an engagement letter, identification of critical or important functions per DORA, and procurement of independent TI and RT providers (or formal internal-tester rotation under DORA Art.27 — see separate finding).
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Preparation phase; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general); RTS on TLPT (general) — scope, providers, critical functions (Memory cite — verify against the published RTS on TLPT.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — actors (TCT, CT, WT, TI, RT); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26/Art.27 (general); RTS on TLPT — control team, communication with authorities (Memory cite — verify.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.27 (general) — requirements for testers (Memory cite — verify against the live regulation and the RTS on TLPT.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — RT provider procurement; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Closure phase — replay, 360° feedback, attestation; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general); RTS on TLPT — closure artefacts and attestation (Memory cite — verify.)
recommendation_text: Add a Preparation section to the methodology covering: GTL consumption, engagement letter template, critical/important-functions identification, and procurement criteria for independent TI and RT providers (or internal-tester arrangements). Verify each item against the live TIBER-EU v2 text and the DORA RTS on TLPT.

title: No purple-teaming close-out with blue team and physical security
evidence_quote_or_observation: "The final stage for the Physical Pentest is to report the outcome of each scenario." The document ends at reporting; there is no joint debrief with the blue team, the SOC, the physical security function or the Local Facilities Manager to translate findings into defensive improvements.
expected: A mandatory purple-team close-out phase that walks blue team, SOC and physical security through what was done, when, and what should have been detected or stopped, with concrete defensive actions agreed.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure phase (replay / 360 feedback) (Closure includes a replay workshop with the blue team.); PTES:PTES Reporting; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure phase artefacts; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 - test summary, remediation plan, attestation (general) (Confidence low - cited from memory.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Purple-team close-out' section after Report that mandates a joint replay with blue team, SOC and physical security covering timeline, IoCs/physical indicators, detection gaps and agreed control improvements.

title: No kill-switch, abort criteria or check-in / emergency-contact protocol for operators on site
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This letter is to be used in a worst case scenario where, during the tests, local personnel get suspicious of the Red Team Operators activities and threaten to take actions, such as calling the police." There is no description of how an operator triggers an abort, who they call if injured, what hours they may operate, or how check-ins back to a control point are scheduled.
expected: A documented operator safety protocol: scheduled check-ins to a named control point, abort/kill-switch wording, emergency contacts (medical, legal, in-country), permitted hours, and escalation if a check-in is missed.
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 Planning - Rules of Engagement (RoE must define communications, safety and abort triggers.); OSSTMM:OSSTMM PHYSSEC Rules of Engagement
recommendation_text: Add a 'Operator safety and check-ins' subsection to Planning specifying: control-point contact, mandatory check-in cadence, missed-check-in escalation, abort trigger words, in-country emergency contacts (medical / legal), permitted hours and clothing/identification.

title: All four TIBER-EU phases are absent for the physical leg
evidence_quote_or_observation: The Physical Pentest pipeline is Planning -> Weaponization -> Execution (Recon / Exploit / Escalation) -> Report. There is no Generic Threat Landscape consumption, no Targeted Threat Intelligence (TTI) report driving the pretexts and objectives, no replay / 360 feedback / attestation / knowledge sharing in Closure.
expected: When the assessment is run under TLPT, physical scenarios are derived from TTI, executed under control-team oversight, and closed with replay and attestation - mirroring the four TIBER-EU phases (Preparation, Threat Intelligence, Red Team Test, Closure).
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Reporting / Post-Engagement; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 Post-Testing Activities; PTES:PTES Post-Engagement; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 four-phase structure (Preparation, Threat Intelligence, Red Team Test, Closure); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 - advanced testing of ICT tools, systems and processes
recommendation_text: Add a TLPT-mode addendum that maps each Physical Pentest stage to the TIBER-EU phases: TTI-derived pretexts and objectives, control-team-supervised execution, replay-and-attestation closure. Keep the internal Maturity flow as the non-TLPT path.

title: Planning is red-team-only; Threat Scenarios require joint planning with the blue team
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'This phase usually begins with a brainstorming session among the operators to discuss all possible scenarios, evaluate the targets features and possible abuse cases.' The blue team / SOC / defenders are not listed as participants in any planning step.
expected: A 'Joint planning with blue team' step inside Planning — the defining feature of the Threat Scenario engagement type per Euronext's taxonomy (assumed-breach with blue team in the room).
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions
recommendation_text: Restructure Planning so that, after the red team's internal brainstorm, a mandatory joint planning workshop is held with the SOC / blue team and the system owner to agree scenarios, success criteria and the demonstration format. Document attendees and outputs.
owner_hint: Red team lead with SOC/Blue team

title: No threat-hunt handover from Threat Scenario findings to the hunt team
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the document has no section on threat hunting, hunt hypotheses, or handover to the hunt team. OPLOGS are described only as a record for 'final report', 'incident response and forensic investigations' and 'training', not as input to a structured hunt.
expected: A handover step where each successful (and unsuccessful-but-instructive) scenario is converted into one or more hunt hypotheses with target data sources, queries / detections to build, and an owner.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Reporting — defensive remediation and detection guidance; PTES:PTES Reporting — remediation roadmap; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §8 — Post-testing activities (remediation); PTES:PTES Reporting — internal knowledge transfer
recommendation_text: Add a 'Threat-hunt handover' subsection (after Purple-team close-out) describing how scenario outcomes translate into hunt hypotheses, what data sources are required, who owns each hunt, and how completed hunts feed back into the engagement record.
owner_hint: Red team lead with Threat hunt

title: Physical scope is not addressed — neither in-scope nor explicitly out-of-scope
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the document references 'critical company tool, application or process' and 'internal tools' but never mentions physical access, badge cloning, tailgating, on-prem datacentre access, or any other physical attack vector. There is no explicit out-of-scope statement either.
expected: An explicit statement that physical attack paths are either in scope (with the safety controls that follow) or out of scope, with a one-line rationale, so a Threat Scenario covering, for example, badge issuance or visitor management is not implicitly excluded.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM Physical Security Channel (Cited from memory.)
recommendation_text: In the Scope subsection, add a one-line statement on physical scope: by default out-of-scope for tool-focused Threat Scenarios, but explicitly in-scope when the scenario subject is a physical control (e.g. badge system, datacentre access, visitor flow).

title: Blast-radius / safety controls for production tool abuse are vague
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'In all steps of this phase, the operators should keep in mind the scope and restrictions defined, in order to not cause unwanted damage to normal company operations.' and 'Before starting this phase, it's important to have a peer review of the scenarios outlined on the previous phase. This is used to understand if the scenarios make sense in the business context and if they can cause damage to regular business operations.' Both rely on operator judgement; there are no concrete blast-radius controls (test accounts, sandboxed projects, time windows, change-window coordination, rollback plan).
expected: Concrete safety controls for Threat Scenarios that touch production internal tools (GitLab, Jenkins, CI/CD, ticketing, secret stores): test accounts/projects, agreed time windows, change-management coordination, rollback plan, and named on-call contact who can pause execution.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions — engagement disruption avoidance; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3.2 — Test logistics and safeguards
recommendation_text: Add a 'Safety controls' subsection in Execution listing: dedicated test accounts/projects where possible, agreed execution windows, the change-management coordination point, a rollback plan and a named on-call contact authorised to pause/abort.
owner_hint: Red team lead with system owner

title: Document does not state that Threat Scenarios are not TLPT, leaving a misalignment risk if cited as DORA evidence
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'This document describes the Red Team methodology that all Red Team members should adhere to when performing a Threat Scenario against a critical company tool, application or process.' — the phrase 'critical company tool' could be misread by a non-specialist as a 'critical or important function' in the DORA sense. The document never disclaims TLPT applicability, and the Execution phases (Reconnaissance, Exploit, Escalation) read like a kill-chain, which could be cited externally as if it were TLPT evidence.
expected: An explicit statement that Threat Scenarios are an internal control-validation activity, not TLPT, and that TLPT obligations under DORA Art.26 / TIBER-EU are met through the Exercise process when applicable. This avoids the audit risk of the doc being misread or selectively quoted.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) — distinction between resilience testing and TLPT (Cited from memory; verify against the live regulation text.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — anti-misalignment: stealth-free walk-through is not TLPT
recommendation_text: Add a single 'Regulatory positioning' sentence in the Introduction: Threat Scenarios are internal control validation, not TLPT; TLPT-scoped activity follows the Exercise process. Cross-reference Exercises and Operations process documents.

title: Operation execution flow lacks the assumed-breach setup phase
evidence_quote_or_observation: This phase involves gaining the initial access or foothold into the target system or network. It typically starts with reconnaissance and vulnerability scanning, followed by exploiting a vulnerability or utilizing a social engineering technique to gain access.
expected: An Operation should start from an assumed-breach foothold provisioned by the white team, then execute pre-defined scenarios toward the objective; full external initial access is the Exercise shape, not the Operation shape.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions / Exploitation (PTES anchors the phase distinction; the Operations shape per Euronext taxonomy is assumed-breach scenario execution.)
recommendation_text: Add an explicit 'Assumed-Breach Setup' phase before Initial Foothold (or replace Initial Foothold) that describes how the WT provisions the foothold, how scenarios are defined up front, and how scenario-driven execution differs from full kill-chain emulation.

title: Codename convention does not require the fictional-character theme
evidence_quote_or_observation: Before starting the next phase, the RT it is required to choose a unique codename for the operation to maintain confidentiality and track the operation internally. The codename should not reveal any information about the target or the techniques being used, to ensure that only authorized personnel have access to the codename and associated documentation.
expected: For Operations, the codename theme must be known fictional characters (movies / TV / books) per the Euronext convention; the SOP should state this explicitly.
framework_refs: OTHER:Euronext red team codename convention (placeholder for internal taxonomy reference) (Convention recorded in schemas/engagement-types.md; awaiting internal taxonomy id.)
recommendation_text: Update the CODENAME section to state that Operation codenames must be drawn from known fictional characters (movies / TV / books), with examples and a register of used names to avoid collisions.

title: No threat-hunt handover from red findings
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing>
expected: Closure should describe how each red finding (TTP, IoC, behavioural signal) is converted into a hunt hypothesis and handed to the threat-hunt function for retro and forward-looking sweeps.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure / knowledge sharing; MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK technique-to-detection / hunt mapping; PTES:PTES Post-Engagement / remediation; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure / remediation planning; PTES:PTES Post-Engagement / lessons learned; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure phase artefacts (Cited from memory.); DORA:DORA operational resilience testing (general) (Cited from memory; verify which DORA articles apply to non-TLPT testing evidence.)
recommendation_text: Add a Threat-Hunt Handover step in Closure: a structured artefact (per-TTP hunt hypothesis, data sources, expected telemetry, time horizon) that is delivered to the hunt team and tracked to closure.

title: Physical scope is mentioned in passing but never explicitly scoped or made optional
evidence_quote_or_observation: It's to simulate Threat Actors Tactics, Techniques and Procedures (TTPs), that include a blended approach (Social Engineering, physical intrusion, application/network pentest)  to reach the pre-defined objective.
expected: The SOP should state explicitly whether physical intrusion is in or out of scope for an Operation, and if in scope point to the physical/social process and questionnaire.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM Physical Security Testing; PTES:PTES Intelligence Gathering / Physical
recommendation_text: Add a Scope subsection that explicitly addresses cyber + physical: state default in/out of scope, link to the Physical/Social process and questionnaire, and require the RoE to record physical scope per engagement.

title: 'White Team' role conflates TIBER-EU White Team and Control Team responsibilities
evidence_quote_or_observation: Link between the RT and the BT, and consists of employees who can escalate and de-escalate with both teams. Informed of all attacks by the Red Team and is mandated to start, stop and approve attacks.
expected: Under TIBER-EU v2, the Control Team (CT) owns the test (start/stop/approve, scope, kill-switch) and the White Team (WT) is the small group of staff at the institution who are aware. They are distinct. If Operations are NOT TLPT, the SOP should still avoid using TIBER-EU terminology in a way that contradicts the framework, so that the same vocabulary works for an Exercise that IS run as TLPT.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 actors: Control Team vs White Team (Cited from memory; verify exact role definitions in TIBER-EU v2.); DORA-TLPT:DORA RTS on TLPT (general) - control team (RTS on TLPT (Article on roles) requires a named control team distinct from the blue team.)
recommendation_text: Either (a) introduce a separate Control Team role with the start/stop/approve authority and reduce White Team to the awareness/communication role per TIBER-EU, or (b) add a glossary note that this SOP uses 'White Team' as a combined CT+WT role for internal Operations and that for TLPT-aligned engagements the two roles are split. Verify TIBER-EU role definitions against the live framework.

title: Codename convention does not require the fictional-character theme
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Before starting the next phase, the RT it is required to choose a unique codename for the operation to maintain confidentiality and track the operation internally. The codename should not reveal any information about the target or the techniques being used'. No mention of the Euronext convention that Exercise codenames be drawn from known fictional characters (movies / TV / books).
expected: The doc should state explicitly that Exercise codenames must be drawn from a 'known fictional characters' theme (movies, TV, books) per the Euronext red team convention, and that the chosen codename must be recorded in the engagement register.
framework_refs: OTHER:Euronext red team naming convention (codename + fictional-character theme) (Internal taxonomy placeholder; convention captured in schemas/engagement-types.md.)
recommendation_text: Update the CODENAME section to require codenames drawn from known fictional characters (movies, TV, books), and to be logged in the engagement register. Provide one or two illustrative examples.

title: Physical scope not explicitly addressed (in or out of scope)
evidence_quote_or_observation: Physical intrusion is referenced only in the Red Teaming definition ('blended approach (Social Engineering, physical intrusion, application/network pentest)') and the Introduction. The Initiation, Requirements, and RoE-related text never confirm whether physical scope is in-scope, out-of-scope, or addressed via a separate authorization.
expected: The Initiation / Requirements / RoE section must explicitly state whether physical scope (premises, badge cloning, tailgating, dropped devices) is in scope, partially in scope, or out of scope, with rationale and any extra authorizations required.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM 3 Physical Security Testing (PHYSSEC); PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions (scope); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26(2)-(3) (scope, critical or important functions, live production) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.)
recommendation_text: Add an explicit 'Physical scope' subsection under Initiation/Requirements. Default to 'must be agreed in writing as in or out of scope per exercise' and list the additional authorizations required if in scope (facilities, HR, building security).

title: Initiation phase blurs scoping and authorization, lacks engagement letter / RoE sign-off gate
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'The output of this Phase should be a clearly defined the scope and limitations of the engagement... include any restrictions on techniques, tools, or attack vectors and the respective locations where these are prohibited.' The Initiation phase is described prose-style; there is no explicit gate (signed engagement letter / RoE) before Targeted TI begins.
expected: Initiation should produce a signed engagement letter and signed RoE as deliverables, and the next phase (Targeted TI) should be gated on those signatures.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Preparation phase (Engagement letter, scoping document)
recommendation_text: Restructure Initiation around named deliverables: scoping document + engagement letter + signed RoE. Make 'all three signed' a hard gate before Targeted TI commences.

title: TIBER-EU Preparation phase elements (GTL, engagement letter, critical-functions identification, provider procurement) only partially present
evidence_quote_or_observation: Initiation describes critical-systems sharing ('the WT will share details about these critical assets, including their configuration, architecture, dependencies') and a scope output, but does not reference: a Generic Threat Landscape (GTL); a formal engagement letter; identification of 'critical or important functions' per DORA; or procurement / qualification of TI and RT providers.
expected: Preparation should produce: (a) consumption of the GTL where one exists, (b) a signed engagement letter, (c) identification of critical or important functions per DORA, (d) defined procurement / qualification criteria for TI and RT providers (especially when external).
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Preparation phase (GTL, scoping, procurement) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (critical or important functions) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.)
recommendation_text: Add explicit Preparation deliverables to the Initiation phase (or to the TLPT addendum): GTL ingestion, engagement letter, critical-or-important-functions list, provider procurement / qualification record. Cite DORA Art.26 and TIBER-EU Preparation when used.

title: Threat Intelligence provider independence not addressed; TI and RT appear conflated
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Threat Intelligence Team (TI) - Responsible for performing Threat Actors investigation' and later 'During this phase, the RT team focuses on gathering information about emerging and current threats'. The TI Team is described as part of the same engagement structure with no separation requirement, and no reference to an external/independent TI provider.
expected: Under TIBER-EU / DORA TLPT, the TI provider must be independent of the RT provider (or the dual-role be explicitly justified and approved). The doc should require a Targeted Threat Intelligence Report from a clearly identified provider, separate from the RT executing team in the TLPT case.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Threat Intelligence phase (TI provider, TTI report) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.27 (tester requirements) and RTS on TLPT (TI provider) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation and RTS text.)
recommendation_text: State explicitly that for TLPT-framed Exercises the TI provider must be independent of the RT provider, with a named Targeted Threat Intelligence Report deliverable. For internal Exercises, document that internal TI is acceptable but separation of responsibilities is recommended.
owner_hint: Threat intel lead + Red team lead + Compliance

title: White Team described, but no Control Team distinct from White Team / no TIBER Cyber Team / TCT engagement
evidence_quote_or_observation: The doc defines RT, TI, BT, WT only. There is no 'Control Team' (CT) — the small institution-side team that owns the test under TIBER-EU and is distinct from the White Team in some implementations — and no reference to a TIBER Cyber Team (TCT) at the regulator. The WT is described as 'employees who can escalate and de-escalate with both teams... Informed of all attacks by the Red Team and is mandated to start, stop and approve attacks'.
expected: For TLPT framing, identify the Control Team (small group inside the institution owning the test) distinct from the White Team where the framework requires it, and the TCT / authority interface. For non-TLPT Exercises, clarify whether WT is intended to fulfil both CT and WT functions and accept the trade-off.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Actors (TCT, Control Team, White Team) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text — TIBER-EU terminology distinguishes WT/CT in places, with the CT typically being the small institution-side team owning the test.); DORA-TLPT:DORA RTS on TLPT (control team, communication with authorities) (Cited from memory; verify against live RTS text.)
recommendation_text: Add (in the TLPT addendum or this SOP): Control Team identification distinct from WT where the framework requires it; named interface to the TCT / lead overseer; explicit naming of the institution-side test owner.

title: No coordination with competent authority / lead overseer described
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> The doc does not describe notification to or coordination with any competent authority, lead overseer, TIBER-XX implementation, or NCA (AFM, AMF, FSMA, CSSF, CONSOB, CMVM). Mutual recognition between authorities (relevant for Euronext's multi-jurisdiction estate) is also unaddressed.
expected: Where the Exercise is a DORA TLPT, the doc should require pre-test notification of the lead overseer / TCT, post-test attestation, and use of the mutual-recognition pathway to reduce duplicate testing across jurisdictions.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT (authority coordination, mutual recognition) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation and RTS text.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Generic Process (TCT engagement) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.); OTHER:TIBER-XX national implementations (AFM, AMF, FSMA, CSSF, CONSOB, CMVM) (Euronext operates regulated entities under multiple NCAs; jurisdictional choice is non-trivial.)
recommendation_text: Add (in TLPT addendum) a section 'Authority coordination': identifying the lead overseer for each in-scope Euronext entity, the notification trigger and channel, the attestation deliverable, and the mutual-recognition workflow. Flag jurisdictional ambiguity where multiple NCAs could claim primacy.

title: Tester requirements (DORA Art.27) not addressed: internal vs external testers, qualifications, rotation
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> The doc says nothing about whether the RT may be internal, external, or mixed; whether internal testers must rotate to maintain independence; or what qualification / certification criteria apply to external testers. Art.27 sets specific tester requirements for TLPT.
expected: Under TLPT framing, the SOP should reference DORA Art.27: external testers must meet defined criteria (suitability, technical capability, indemnity); internal testers are permitted under additional conditions (rotation, independence, no use of internal testers for the same critical function repeatedly).
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.27 (Requirements for testers) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Procurement Guidelines (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Tester requirements' subsection citing DORA Art.27: criteria for external testers; conditions for using internal testers (rotation, independence); approval workflow when internal testers are used for a TLPT.

title: Risk-management of the test itself underspecified: no kill-switch, no safe-harbours / indemnities, no rollback plan
evidence_quote_or_observation: The doc has data-handling halts and a deconfliction process (good), but it does not name an explicit kill-switch, safe-harbours / indemnities for testers (legal protection while operating in live prod), or a rollback / restoration plan if a test action causes operational impact.
expected: TLPT framing requires explicit risk-management of the test: kill-switch authority, safe-harbour clauses for testers, abort criteria, rollback / restoration plan, and pre-agreed crisis-comms playbook.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT (risk management of the test, safe harbours) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation and RTS text.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Risk Management Guidance (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Risk management of the test' subsection (in the TLPT addendum and this SOP): kill-switch and abort authority; safe-harbour / indemnity clauses for internal and external testers; rollback procedure; crisis-comms plan if a test triggers a real incident response.

title: No formal authorization / RoE for continuous AD enumeration, only an informal SOC heads-up
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'This data collection will collect all available information and SOC team should be warned of the recurrent collection, and disregard any alerts during this time coming from the SharpHound Enterprise Server.'
expected: A documented authorization chain (sign-off owner, scope of domains/forests in scope, change-control reference, abort criteria) and written agreement with the SOC to suppress/whitelist alerts, rather than an informal warning to disregard alerts.
framework_refs: PTES:Pre-engagement Interactions — Authorization and Scope; NIST-800-115:§3.4 Rules of Engagement / Authorization
recommendation_text: Reference (or include) an authorization template covering: which domains and OUs are in scope for SharpHound enumeration, the named approver, the formal SOC suppression/allowlisting agreement (not 'disregard alerts'), kill-switch / pause procedure, and reauthorization cadence.
owner_hint: Red team lead + SOC lead + Legal/Risk

title: No data-handling, retention or classification rules for collected AD/Entra graph data
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'This information is then uploaded to Euronext tenant to be analysed and attack paths mapped' and 'Access to Euronext BloodHound tenant is fully managed by AET, and access to it is restricted to members of the team.' No mention of data classification, retention period, deletion criteria, GDPR basis, or what is done with the collected directory snapshot when an engagement closes.
expected: Explicit rules on classification (the AD graph is highly sensitive), retention period of collection snapshots, deletion at end of engagement, GDPR / personal-data handling basis, and tenant-side encryption / region of storage.
framework_refs: PTES:Pre-engagement Interactions — Information Handling; DORA:Art. 5–6 ICT risk management — protection of ICT assets and data (AD topology data is itself an ICT asset under DORA's risk-management duties.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Data Handling' section: classify BHE collection data, define retention (e.g. snapshots purged N days after engagement close), state where the SaaS tenant stores data and the GDPR basis, and require Legal/Risk sign-off for any external sharing of attack-path graphs.

title: Tenant access uses application accounts only; SAML/SSO and MFA not yet implemented
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'As of writing this document, SAML is not yet implemented, and only application accounts are used. If access to the tool is required, one of the administrators in AET should be contacted to create the account.'
expected: SAML/SSO with the corporate IdP, MFA enforced, joiners/movers/leavers tied to the IdP, periodic access reviews, and break-glass account procedure for the SaaS tenant that hosts the entire AD attack-path graph.
framework_refs: DORA:Art. 9 — protection and prevention; identity & access management for ICT assets; NIST-800-115:§3.4 — secure handling of test tooling and data
recommendation_text: Track SAML/SSO + MFA enablement as a remediation item with an owner and date; in the meantime document compensating controls (named local accounts, password vaulting, enforced MFA at the app layer, quarterly access review) and a break-glass procedure.

title: Remediation section names no owner, no tracking system, no severity model and no SLA
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'When providing Remediation plan to the proper teams, it is required to give a short explanation on what is the Edge being reported and why it is being reported. ... Most of the Edges are self-explanatory, and remediation should be easy to remove the abusive permission. If this is not the case, it should be reviewed on a case-by-case basis, by the team, on the proper recommendation to give.'
expected: A defined recipient (AD owner / IAM team), a ticket / register where each finding is tracked, a severity model (e.g. Tier 0 paths = critical), an SLA, and a re-test mechanism via the next BHE collection.
framework_refs: PTES:Reporting — Findings, Risk Rating, Remediation; NIST-800-115:§7 — Reporting and remediation
recommendation_text: Define a remediation workflow: who receives findings, in which ticketing system, severity per Tier-0 reachability, SLA per severity, and an explicit re-test loop using the next scheduled BHE collection to verify closure.

title: No purple-team close-out / blue-team handover for BHE-derived findings
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the document describes BHE analysis and remediation to 'the proper teams' but never names a blue/SOC purple-team session to walk through attack paths, validate detections (e.g. detection of SharpHound itself, of AddMember to Tier-0), or co-design controls.
expected: A mandatory purple-team session: red walks blue through prioritised Tier-0 paths, blue confirms detection coverage (SharpHound enumeration, ADCS abuse, ForceChangePassword, etc.), and gaps feed into detection engineering.
framework_refs: PTES:Post-Exploitation / Reporting — knowledge transfer; MITRE-ATTACK:TA0006 Credential Access / TA0008 Lateral Movement / T1098 Account Manipulation (BHE edges map onto these techniques and should drive detection engineering.); PTES:Post-Exploitation — handover to defenders; MITRE-ATTACK:Hunt-driven detection on technique catalogue; PTES:Reporting — knowledge capture; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — Red Team Test phase tooling and Closure artefacts; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art. 26–27 (general) — RTS on TLPT (control team, attestation, evidence retention) (Verify exact RTS clause numbers against the live text.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Purple-team Handover' subsection requiring a debrief with SOC/Detection Engineering after each BHE remediation cycle: review prioritised attack paths, verify detection coverage for SharpHound collection and the abused edges, and log detection gaps into the backlog.
owner_hint: Red team lead + Purple team / Detection Engineering

merged_finding_ids: lifecycle-rtmeth-002; lifecycle-rtmeth-005; lifecycle-rtmeth-006; lifecycle-rtmeth-008; lifecycle-rtmeth-009; lifecycle-rtmeth-020
title: Codename convention (mandatory codename, fictional-character theme for Exercises and Operations) is absent
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the methodology contains no mention of codename assignment, naming convention, or codename register. Threat Profile examples (Autobank, Blueheart, Cybersnake, Simplesam) are profile names, not engagement codenames.
expected: The methodology must require a codename for every engagement and prescribe the fictional-character theme (movies/TV/books) for Exercises and Operations, while noting Threat Scenarios use a separate convention.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Preparation phase (Engagement identification artefacts (e.g. engagement letter) are part of Preparation.); MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK TTP mapping (general) (Hunt hypotheses are typically expressed against ATT&CK techniques.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Closure — knowledge sharing; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 Post-test activities; DORA:DORA Art.17 (ICT-related incident management) — supporting role (Memory cite — verify against live text.); PTES:PTES Reporting; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 Reporting; MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK Navigator / Technique mapping; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Closure — remediation planning; PTES:PTES Post-engagement; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — actors (TCT, CT, WT, TI, RT); PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions — Roles
recommendation_text: Add a 'Codename Assignment' section to the methodology stating: every engagement (Exercise/Operation/Threat Scenario) is assigned a codename before authorization; Exercises and Operations follow the fictional-character theme; Threat Scenarios follow the team's alternative convention. Reference the codename register.

title: Purple-teaming close-out described as conditional collaboration, not as a mandatory close-out for every engagement
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'These collaboration can happen as the output of each of the other type of Red Team engagements (Threat Scenarios, Operations or Exercises)…' — phrasing is permissive ('can happen'), not mandatory.
expected: The methodology should state unambiguously that a purple-team close-out (replay + control mapping with the blue team) is mandatory at the close of every Exercise, Operation and Threat Scenario.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Closure — replay / 360° feedback (TIBER mandates a replay step as part of Closure.); PTES:PTES Reporting / Post-engagement
recommendation_text: Replace 'can happen' with explicit mandate: 'A purple-team close-out is required at the end of every Exercise, Operation and Threat Scenario. The Purple Teaming Process document defines the replay format, attendee list, alert/log correlation matrix, and improvement backlog.'

title: Physical scope not addressed in the methodology despite being explicitly in the team's remit
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — apart from a single mention of 'physical access to buildings/equipment' in the Threat Perspective table, the methodology does not address physical engagement scope, controls, or rules. There is no cross-reference to the PhysicalSocial process.
expected: The methodology should state that physical scope is in remit, summarise the controls (authorization escalation, badge/photo handling, safety, on-site coordination), and cross-reference the PhysicalSocial process document.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM Physical Security Testing channel; PTES:PTES Intelligence Gathering / Physical
recommendation_text: Add a 'Physical scope' subsection that confirms physical assessments are in scope, identifies the additional sign-offs required (site/facility owner, Legal), and references the PhysicalSocial Process document and questionnaire.

title: Scoping section is a 4-bullet capability checklist; lacks asset/criticality scoping, exclusions, and target selection method
evidence_quote_or_observation: Scoping section in full: 'Can the Red Team perform these actions / Access to zero-days? / Does the team have the required skills/knowledge to emulate an attack in the same timeframe. / Scope the engagement with same staff and time parameters'. It addresses team capability, not what is in scope.
expected: A Scoping section in a methodology should describe how scope is set: critical/important functions, in-scope and out-of-scope assets/networks/identities, time windows, blue-team awareness, and the scoping artefact that captures it.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Preparation — scoping document; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) — critical/important functions in scope; PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions — Scope
recommendation_text: Rewrite the Scoping section to describe how scope is determined and recorded: identification of critical/important functions, in-scope and explicitly out-of-scope assets, identities and networks, time windows, blue-team-awareness rule per engagement type, and scoping document template. The current 'capability checklist' content should be moved to a separate 'Engagement feasibility' subsection.

title: Methodology calls Exercises 'TIBER-Like' without committing to the TIBER-EU phase model or DORA TLPT requirements
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Red Team Exercises, are full Red Team engagements, well-planned and TIBER-Like.' The doc mentions TIBER once and never uses the words TIBER-EU, TLPT, DORA, control team, white team, threat intelligence provider, attestation, lead overseer, mutual recognition, or competent authority.
expected: If Exercises are positioned as TIBER-EU / DORA TLPT, the methodology must adopt the TIBER-EU phase model (Preparation, TI, RTT, Closure) and DORA TLPT actor model. If they are not, the doc must say so plainly to avoid implying regulator alignment that does not exist.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — phase model; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) — advanced testing based on TLPT
recommendation_text: Replace 'TIBER-Like' with one of two precise positions: (a) Exercises are run as TIBER-EU / DORA TLPT and follow the prescribed phases, actors, and artefacts (cross-reference per-NCA TIBER-XX guidance); or (b) Exercises are inspired by TIBER-EU but do not satisfy DORA Art.26 / TLPT — and remove any implication that they do. Verify against the live TIBER-EU v2 framework text and the relevant national TIBER-XX implementation.

title: Threat Intelligence phase: no Targeted Threat Intelligence (TTI) report, no independent TI provider, no scenario-derivation method
evidence_quote_or_observation: Threat Planning lists guiding questions and four legacy threat-profile examples (Autobank, Blueheart, Cybersnake, Simplesam — clearly inspired by SCYTHE / public material) without a TTI report deliverable, without an independent TI provider, and without explaining how Euronext-specific scenarios are derived for a given engagement.
expected: TIBER-EU requires a Targeted Threat Intelligence report produced by an independent TI provider (or by a function that meets equivalent independence under DORA). The methodology must require this artefact and describe scenario derivation from TTI.
framework_refs: MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK Enterprise — Tactics & Techniques; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Threat Intelligence — TTI report; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26/Art.27 (general); RTS on TLPT — TI provider requirements (Memory cite — verify.)
recommendation_text: Add a Threat Intelligence phase that requires a Targeted Threat Intelligence report, names the provider/function (with independence criteria), and shows how TTI drives scenario selection. Replace or supplement the legacy threat profiles with Euronext-specific, FMI-sector-relevant TTPs.

title: Risk management of the test itself: no kill-switch, no rollback / safe-harbour clauses, no formal indemnity for testers
evidence_quote_or_observation: Forbidden Attacks states DoS-causing attacks are 'strictly forbidden' and that exploits should be lab-tested first. There is no kill-switch, no escalation/abort criteria, no rollback procedure for persistence implants, and no safe-harbour / indemnity language for testers.
expected: TIBER-EU requires risk management of the test itself: kill-switch, rollback, safe harbours and indemnities. DORA mandates that the test does not jeopardise the institution's resilience.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — RTT risk management; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general); RTS on TLPT — controlled execution (Memory cite — verify.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Test risk management' subsection: kill-switch ownership, abort criteria, rollback expectations for persistence/registry artefacts, safe-harbour / indemnity language for testers. Reference the RoE template.

title: Data Handling references US 'Privacy of Information Act (PIA)' — no GDPR / DORA legal basis cited
evidence_quote_or_observation: '…will not intentionally compromise Privacy of Information Act (PIA), medical, justice, worship or religious pursuit, or any other protected or privileged information that may fall into one of the following categories…' followed by 'Personal confidential data / Business confidential data / Financial confidential data / Regulated data'.
expected: An EU-regulated FMI's red team methodology must cite GDPR (and applicable national data-protection law) as the legal basis for handling personal/confidential data encountered during testing, plus any DORA / TIBER-EU data-handling expectations. References to a US-style 'Privacy of Information Act' are incorrect for the operating jurisdictions.
framework_refs: DORA:DORA — data handling during testing (general) (Memory cite — verify.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — data protection during the test; OTHER:GDPR (Reg. (EU) 2016/679) Art.5/Art.6/Art.9 — lawful basis and special categories
recommendation_text: Replace the 'Privacy of Information Act (PIA)' reference with GDPR (and the applicable national data-protection law where engagements occur) and align the data categories with the lawful-basis and special-category framing. Where engagements are TIBER-EU framed, add a reference to the framework's data-handling expectations.

title: Scoping does not reference DORA 'critical or important functions' nor commit to live-production testing under controls
evidence_quote_or_observation: Scoping section reads in full: 'Can the Red Team perform these actions / Access to zero-days? / Does the team have the required skills/knowledge to emulate an attack in the same timeframe. / Scope the engagement with same staff and time parameters'. No reference to critical/important functions, in-scope production systems, or DORA-driven scope criteria.
expected: TIBER-EU and DORA TLPT require scope to be tied to the institution's critical or important functions and to include live production systems under controls. The methodology must reference this concept and describe how scope is mapped to those functions.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general); RTS on TLPT — scope based on critical/important functions (Memory cite — verify.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) Preparation — scoping to critical functions
recommendation_text: Rewrite Scoping to reference Euronext's register of critical / important functions, describe how scope is derived from them, and confirm that production systems are in scope under controlled conditions. Verify the wording against DORA Art.26 and the RTS on TLPT.

section_ref: Process Overview / Deep Dive Research / Capability Development
title: Hypothesis-to-validation lifecycle lacks an explicit lab/sandbox environment and authorization gate
evidence_quote_or_observation: "The assigned operator must reproduce and validate the techniques, perform environment-specific testing and analysis of detection and control impacts." The text says "environment-specific testing" but never names a lab, sandbox, isolated tenant, or non-production network; nor does it describe an authorization gate before techniques are reproduced.
expected: Explicit requirement that reproduction and validation happen in a named isolated lab/sandbox, with an authorization gate (and pre-approval for any test that touches prod-like infrastructure or customer-representative data).
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 (Planning) and §4 (Execution) - test environment and authorization (NIST 800-115 requires test environments and written authorization to be established before technique execution.); PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions - Authorization & test environment
recommendation_text: Add a 'Lab & Authorization' subsection requiring techniques be first reproduced in a named isolated lab, with documented authorization (and a stricter gate for any reproduction that needs prod-like or production data).

title: No legal / IP / responsible-disclosure review for capabilities derived from external research
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This step must output either a new tool, PoC or enhance existing tooling." and "New capabilities developed must be added on the RT Tool Repository, with clear documentation and usage guidance, linked research references, OPSEC and detection notes." No mention of legal review, IP / licensing checks on third-party code, vendor-side responsible disclosure, or restrictions on publishing capabilities.
expected: Explicit IP / licensing / legal-review checkpoint before a capability is committed to the Tool Repository, plus a responsible-disclosure decision (notify vendor / coordinate publication) when a 0-day or non-public weakness is involved.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM Rules of Engagement - Legality & Disclosure (OSSTMM requires that legality of methods and disclosure handling be agreed before capability is used.); OTHER:ISO/IEC 29147 (Vulnerability disclosure) (Reference standard for coordinated/responsible disclosure when research touches third-party products.); PTES:PTES Reporting / Tooling maintenance (PTES expects tooling to be maintained and decommissioned in a controlled way.)
recommendation_text: Add a Legal/IP review gate to Capability Development covering third-party-code licensing, customer/vendor disclosure obligations, and a documented decision to publish, embargo, or keep internal.

title: Handover from Research to Engagement playbooks is implicit and not gated
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This is the general Red Team Process that is followed in parallel with the research efforts documented in this process. Following this documentation, the RT Operators should consume the research knowledge contained in the Research Repository and use the tools documented in the Tools Repository as described above." There is no formal handover artefact, no requirement that Exercise/Operation/Threat-Scenario playbooks be updated when a Capability lifecycle state changes, and no acceptance criteria for promoting Experimental -> Operational.
expected: An explicit research-to-engagement handover step: criteria to promote a tool from Experimental to Operational (lab evidence, OPSEC sign-off, detection notes), and a requirement to update engagement playbooks / catalogues when new tradecraft is released.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Post Exploitation / Reporting - tradecraft maintenance (PTES expects documented, repeatable tradecraft fed into engagements.); MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK technique mapping as the canonical handover schema
recommendation_text: Define promotion criteria from Experimental -> Operational and require a 'tradecraft release note' that updates Exercise / Operation / Threat-Scenario playbooks and the ATT&CK technique map when a new capability lands.

title: Research methodology silent on threat-hunt hypothesis generation as a deliberate output
evidence_quote_or_observation: "During this step the operators must analyse what worked and what didn't work, identifying where the tools failed and where there was a knowledge gap that needs to be addressed in future research." No explicit step that turns Deep-Dive findings or Capability detections into threat-hunt hypotheses for the blue team.
expected: Explicit step where Deep-Dive notes (Detection considerations) and capability OPSEC notes are converted into threat-hunt hypotheses and lodged with blue team / threat-hunt function.
framework_refs: MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK / TTP-driven hunting model (Hunt hypotheses are conventionally generated from technique-level research.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure - knowledge sharing (Knowledge sharing with defenders is a regulator-aligned pattern.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Threat-hunt hypothesis output' artefact to Deep Dive and Capability Development; require it to be shared with the threat-hunt function on a defined cadence.

title: Adjacent remit not addressed: research input to IR runbooks and to threat-hunting during external tests / live incidents
evidence_quote_or_observation: Scope mentions "Incidents or control observations" as an input but the process body never describes how incident learnings flow into Research, nor how Research outputs are fed into IR runbooks, into threat-hunt support during external tests run against the institution, or into threat-hunt support during live incidents. <missing> for these three adjacent-remit flows.
expected: Explicit description of how research outputs feed IR runbooks, support threat hunting during external red team tests against the institution, and support threat hunting during live incidents.
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 (Post-Testing) - feedback into operational defence (Captures the expectation that test outputs feed defensive operations.); OTHER:NIST SP 800-61r2 (IR) - playbook updates from offensive research
recommendation_text: Add a section describing the three adjacent-remit flows: research -> IR runbook updates, research / RT support during external assessments, research / RT support during live incidents - including triggers and owners.

title: Physical/social process is not linked to the Exercise / Operation / Threat-Scenario engagement taxonomy
evidence_quote_or_observation: The document defines its own assessment ladder (Questionnaire / Physical Audit / Physical Pentest) keyed to a criticality table, but never states how a Physical Pentest plugs into an Exercise, an Operation or a Threat Scenario, or how a physical leg can be embedded in any of them. A confusing 'Figure 1 - Threat Scenarios Phases' label appears under the 'Physical Pentest' heading without explanation: "This type of assessment should mimic a real threat actor attempting to breach the location premisses to achieve their objectives without being detected. To achieve this the Operators should adhere to the process described in this Section / Figure 1 - Threat Scenarios Phases".
expected: An explicit section that states when physical and social engineering is layered into an Exercise, when it is run as a stand-alone Operation, and when it is demonstrated as part of a Threat Scenario, with the corresponding stealth posture for each.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions / Intelligence Gathering (PTES treats physical as a vector inside the same lifecycle as cyber engagements, not a parallel track.); OSSTMM:OSSTMM 3 - Physical Security Testing (PHYSSEC) (OSSTMM positions physical channel testing inside the wider security testing methodology.); NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3.3 Legal Considerations; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 - cross-border / mutual recognition (general) (Confidence low - quoting from memory; verify.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'Relationship to engagement types' section that maps Physical Pentest / Audit / Questionnaire to Exercise, Operation and Threat Scenario, clarifies the stealth posture in each, and removes or relabels the 'Figure 1 - Threat Scenarios Phases' caption so it does not imply a Threat Scenario when the section describes a stealthy Pentest.

title: No codename assignment or fictional-character theme requirement
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> - the word 'codename' does not appear anywhere in the document; planning, scope, weaponization, execution and report sections never reference an engagement codename or naming convention.
expected: An explicit step in Planning that assigns a codename per the Euronext convention (known fictional characters from movies / TV / books for Exercises and Operations) before authorization is signed.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions
recommendation_text: Add a 'Codename' subsection to Planning requiring assignment of a unique codename per the team's theme convention and recording it on the Get-out-of-jail letter and the engagement register.

title: Authorization chain is limited to Local CISO + Local Facilities Manager - no Legal, HR, Group Security or law-enforcement liaison
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This is a formal letter that is signed and agreed upon by both the person authorizing the tests (usually the Local CISO) and a person that is recognizable by Local Facilities teams (usually the Local Facilities Manager)." No mention of Legal sign-off, HR notification, Group Security, building landlord (in shared premises) or a pre-notification to local law-enforcement / national CSIRT where required.
expected: A documented multi-party authorization chain that involves at minimum Legal, HR (operators are employees acting in role), Group Security / Risk, and a recorded notification path to landlord/building security and, where mandated, local law enforcement liaison.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Preparation - White Team / Control Team composition; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3.3 Legal Considerations; OSSTMM:OSSTMM HUMSEC / PHYSSEC operator controls; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 Rules of Engagement; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 - White Team and Control Team roles; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT - Control Team and communications with authorities (general) (Confidence low - quoted from memory; verify the live RTS clause.)
recommendation_text: Expand authorization to require: Legal counter-signature, HR acknowledgement, Group Security awareness, landlord/building-security notification where premises are shared, and a documented decision on local law-enforcement pre-notification per jurisdiction.

title: No social-engineering rules: targeting limits, pretext approval, recording legality, executive carve-outs
evidence_quote_or_observation: "If no weaknesses in the technical controls and processes are found, the Operators will move to Social Engineering techniques, and test the general awareness of the location's employees. This might include Social Engineer the reception workers or other company employees to trick them and enter with their approval." No statement on out-of-scope targets (executives, works council members, persons known to be vulnerable), no pretext-approval workflow, no rule on whether audio/video may be recorded covertly, and no jurisdictional check on the legality of one-party / two-party consent.
expected: A 'Social engineering rules' subsection covering: which categories of staff are out-of-scope without separate sign-off, mandatory pre-approval of pretexts, explicit policy on covert audio/video recording with per-jurisdiction legal check, and post-engagement deletion rules.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM HUMSEC channel; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3.3 Legal Considerations; OSSTMM:OSSTMM PHYSSEC scope and safety rules
recommendation_text: Add a Social-engineering rules subsection covering target carve-outs, pretext approval, audio/video recording legality per jurisdiction, sock-puppet account governance and post-engagement decommissioning.

title: No data-handling rules for material acquired during the engagement (photos, building maps, credentials, badge clones)
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This might include card cloning, lock picking, window climbing, tailgating, or just simply abusing weak access controls" and "This might include connecting a rogue device to exposed ethernet ports, dropping rubber duckies or installing any other type of implants at the location". The document does not say how cloned card data, photos of internal areas, internal documents seen on desks, or captured credentials are stored, encrypted, retained or destroyed.
expected: A data-handling section: encryption-at-rest of photos and captured creds, retention windows, secure destruction at engagement close, GDPR lawful-basis statement (documenting that personal data may incidentally be captured).
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 - data protection during testing; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT - data protection of test artefacts (general) (Confidence low - cite from memory; verify against published RTS.); NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 Test Data Handling; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 risk management of the test; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT - risk management of the test (general) (Confidence low - cited from memory.); NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 Cleanup
recommendation_text: Add a 'Data handling and evidence custody' subsection prescribing encryption, access control, retention, secure destruction, and GDPR lawful-basis text for material captured during physical/social testing.

title: Physical and social engineering is run as a parallel track, not integrated with TIBER-EU / DORA TLPT
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> - the document never mentions TIBER, TIBER-EU, TLPT, DORA, control team, white team, threat intelligence provider, attestation, lead overseer or competent authority. Physical assessments are keyed to an internal Maturity / Criticality model only.
expected: An explicit statement of the relationship to TIBER-EU and DORA TLPT: that physical attack vectors are an allowed leg of a TLPT, and how a Physical Pentest plugs into the same TI-driven scenarios, control-team oversight and attestation pathway as the cyber leg.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 - allowed attack vectors include physical and social engineering (general) (Confidence low - cited from memory; verify against the live TIBER-EU v2 framework text.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT - scope of TLPT covers people/processes, not only technology (general) (Confidence low - cited from memory; verify against published RTS on TLPT.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.27 - requirements for testers; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 - RT provider procurement; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 mutual recognition / cross-border (general) (Confidence low - cited from memory; verify with current TIBER-EU mutual-recognition guidance.); DORA-TLPT:DORA RTS on TLPT - cross-border / lead-authority designation (general) (Confidence low - cited from memory; verify against published RTS.)
recommendation_text: Add a 'TIBER-EU / DORA TLPT alignment' section that (a) confirms physical and social vectors are eligible legs of a TLPT, (b) specifies that when run under TLPT the TI-derived scenarios drive the physical pretexts and objectives, and (c) routes the physical leg through the same control-team / white-team and attestation pathway as the cyber leg. Verify the exact framework text before publication.

title: No Targeted Threat Intelligence input drives physical scenarios
evidence_quote_or_observation: "Before carrying out the execution of the Physical Penetration test, the Operators need to prepare the physical hardware and social engineering scenarios. This includes, but not limited to, having hardware implants to leave on the office location attached to ethernet ports, Rubber Duckies to leave around the office, card cloning technologies to test the access controls used by employees." Scenarios are picked from a generic toolbox; there is no TTI-driven actor profile, no link to specific threat-actor TTPs, and no reference to a TI provider.
expected: Under TLPT, physical and social scenarios are derived from a Targeted Threat Intelligence report identifying credible threat actors and their TTPs. Even outside TLPT, anchoring scenarios to specific threat actors strengthens the audit trail.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Threat Intelligence phase / TTI report; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26-27 - threat intelligence provider role (general) (Confidence low - quoted from memory.); MITRE-ATTACK:MITRE ATT&CK ICS / Enterprise Initial Access (e.g. T1200 Hardware Additions, T1078 Valid Accounts)
recommendation_text: Add a step that, when running under TLPT, the physical scenarios and pretexts are derived from a TTI report from an independent TI provider and mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques.

title: No GDPR lawful-basis statement for personal data captured during physical and social testing
evidence_quote_or_observation: "They will also try to understand the employees' habits to find weaknesses in the controls and processes in places." and "Sock Puppet accounts and interact with employees of the location". Surveillance of employees, recording of social interactions and capture of personal data are foreseeable, but the document carries no GDPR lawful-basis statement, no DPIA reference and no retention/destruction rule.
expected: A GDPR section documenting the lawful basis (e.g. legitimate interests with appropriate safeguards), reference to a DPIA, retention windows, secure destruction and the position on covert audio/video recording per jurisdiction.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 - data protection during TLPT (general) (Confidence low - quoted from memory; verify.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 - data protection considerations; OTHER:GDPR (EU) 2016/679 Articles 6, 35
recommendation_text: Add a GDPR / data-protection section covering lawful basis, DPIA, retention, deletion and per-jurisdiction position on recording. Engage the DPO before next physical test.

title: No questionnaire owner, distribution authority, or response custodian identified
evidence_quote_or_observation: "The Physical Questionnaire is periodically sent to all offices depending on their critically according to the company CMDB." — the document never names who issues it, who reviews responses, who escalates findings, or where responses are stored.
expected: A named role (e.g. Red Team Lead or Physical Security Lead) for issuing, receiving, validating and retaining responses, plus a defined storage location (system + access control) and review SLA.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement — roles, responsibilities and contacts (Pre-engagement scoping requires named owners and points of contact.); OSSTMM:OSSTMM Process — Posture Review (custody of evidence); OSSTMM:OSSTMM Risk Assessment Values (RAV) — reproducible, parameterised scoring
recommendation_text: Add an 'Ownership and Custody' section naming the questionnaire owner, the office-side respondent role, the central reviewer, the storage repository (with access controls), and the response SLA.

title: No data-protection / GDPR handling for questionnaire responses
evidence_quote_or_observation: Responses include physical-security postures, badge photos ("Evidence required must include a photo of the badge or identification method"), CCTV diagrams, access-log samples and floor plans — yet the document is silent on legal basis, redaction, transit security, retention, and access control. <missing>
expected: A 'Data Handling' clause stating: legal basis under GDPR for collecting respondent and third-party data, redaction guidance for visitor logs and badge photos, encrypted transit, named storage location with access control, and a retention/destruction schedule.
framework_refs: DORA:DORA Art.5 — ICT risk management framework, including information security and data confidentiality controls; OTHER:GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) Art.5, 6, 32 — lawful basis, data minimisation, security of processing (Verify against the live regulation text and Euronext's Data Protection Officer guidance.)
recommendation_text: Add a Data Handling section: state the lawful basis, require redaction of personal data in evidence (e.g. blur badge photos, mask names in access logs), mandate encrypted transit, define storage system + access control, and set a retention/destruction schedule consistent with the parent PhysicalSocial process.

title: No risk-screen for regulated environments, trading floors, customer-facing or vulnerable-people areas
evidence_quote_or_observation: The Guidance section enumerates Public/Private/Secure zones, Reception, CCTV, Power, IDS, etc., but never asks whether the location hosts regulated trading systems, customer-facing services, market-data infrastructure, or vulnerable individuals (e.g. visitors with accessibility needs). <missing>
expected: Dedicated questions surfacing regulator-relevant risk: trading-floor presence, market-data rooms, customer-visit frequency, presence of vulnerable people, criticality under DORA / financial-market regulation — used to drive sign-off escalation.
framework_refs: DORA:DORA Art.8 — identification of critical or important functions (Physical sites supporting critical or important functions warrant elevated scrutiny.); OSSTMM:OSSTMM Human Security and Physical Security channels — context-driven scoping; PTES:PTES Intelligence Gathering — Physical, target footprinting feeds into engagement scoping; DORA:DORA Art.5 — record-keeping and traceability of ICT risk management activities
recommendation_text: Add a 'Site Context' question block covering: hosts critical/important DORA function (Y/N), trading or market-data infrastructure on-site, customer-facing footprint, presence of vulnerable people, and any regulator-mandated controls. Use the answers to gate sign-off.

title: No authorization / sign-off chain triggered by questionnaire responses
evidence_quote_or_observation: The document explains that vulnerabilities are opened and severity is increased above 100 employees, but does not state who must approve the response, who countersigns when a critical vulnerability is identified, or how findings are escalated to local site management. <missing>
expected: An authorization matrix: site manager attests to the response, Physical Security Lead reviews, Red Team Lead approves, and any 'Critical' finding triggers escalation to Risk and a tracked remediation owner.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement — written authorisation and escalation path; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3.4 — Rules of Engagement and approvals
recommendation_text: Add an 'Approvals and Escalation' section with a sign-off matrix (site, security, red team, risk) and a rule that any Critical or High finding is escalated immediately rather than waiting for periodic review.

title: CODENAME section is positioned after Scope/Threat Actors/Scenarios but states it must be chosen 'before starting the planning phase'
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Before starting the planning phase, it is required to choose a unique codename for the operation to maintain confidentiality and track the operation internally.' — yet the CODENAME subsection is the fourth and last subsection of Planning, after Scope, Threat Actors and Scenarios Description.
expected: Either reorder the subsection so codename selection appears as the first Planning step, or rephrase the text so the timing matches the document layout (e.g. 'During the planning phase, before scope is finalised…').
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement — engagement identifiers
recommendation_text: Move 'CODENAME' to be the first subsection of Planning and add a sentence on where the codename register lives (which document, who has access).

title: OPLOG format is suggested rather than required, and storage / access controls are not specified
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'As an example, members can use One Note to keep all this information as seen in the example in Figure 2. Keep in mind that each member can choose the best note-keeping format for them selves, as long as the same information is stored.'
