expected: A canonical OPLOG storage location with access controls, retention rules and a defined export format so logs can be reliably handed to IR / forensics — especially given the doc explicitly notes OPLOGS may be used 'during incident response and forensic investigations'.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Reporting — evidence handling; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §4 — Documentation
recommendation_text: Mandate a single OPLOG storage location (or a small approved set), specify access controls, retention period and a standard export schema (CSV/JSON) so logs are usable by IR. Personal note-taking apps remain acceptable as scratch but the canonical OPLOG must live in the approved location.

title: Execution phases (Reconnaissance / Exploit / Escalation) read as a stealth kill-chain rather than a demonstrative walk-through
evidence_quote_or_observation: Execution is structured as 'Reconnaissance → Exploit → Escalation' with the Escalation goal stated as 'trying to get as further as possible within the environment as possible, to achieve maximum impact'. There is no mention of joint discussion with the blue team during execution, real-time detection conversation, or pause-points to demonstrate controls.
expected: Per Euronext's taxonomy, Threat Scenario execution should be a demonstration of internal-tool abuse paths with real-time discussion of detections and controls — i.e. defined pause-points where red walks blue through what was just done and what was/should have been observed.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Vulnerability Analysis / Exploitation / Post-Exploitation (PTES phase names borrowed from the doc; the issue is the framing for a Threat Scenario, not the phases themselves.)
recommendation_text: Reframe Execution to make the demonstrative nature explicit: keep Recon/Exploit/Escalation but add 'demonstration walk-through points' where the operator pauses to show the blue team the artefacts (logs, telemetry, alerts) that did or did not fire. Make clear this is not a stealth kill-chain.
owner_hint: Red team lead with Purple team

title: Report section lacks a finding taxonomy / severity model and evidence requirements
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'In this report, there should also be information regarding the vulnerabilities found, similar to a regular Pentesting report, with severity, description, impact and meaningful recommendations that are relevant and applicable to the business.' — 'similar to a regular Pentesting report' is the only definition; no severity scale, no evidence-attachment requirement, no template reference.
expected: Either an inline severity model (e.g. CVSS or a documented internal scale) or an explicit reference to the team's reporting template, plus minimum evidence requirements (screenshots / OPLOG excerpts / artefact references) per finding.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Reporting; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §7 — Reporting; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — Closure phase: replay, 360° feedback, remediation plan, attestation (Cited as analogous practice; not invoking TLPT applicability. Cited from memory.); DORA:DORA Art.24 (general) — testing programme: documented results and remediation (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.)
recommendation_text: Reference the team's reporting template explicitly and document the severity scale used. Specify the minimum evidence required per finding (screenshot, OPLOG line(s), artefact path).

title: Clean-up obligation is in the Report section and is conditional ('as appropriate'), not a controlled close-out gate
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Finally, any test accounts, dropped files or other modifications which were created should be disabled or removed, as appropriate, together with any associated content.'
expected: Clean-up framed as a mandatory gate at the end of Execution (not buried in Report) with a checklist of artefact types to remove (test accounts, dropped files, persistence, registry/config changes, scheduled jobs), an owner, and a sign-off that it has been completed.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Post-Exploitation — clean-up; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 — Post-Test Activities
recommendation_text: Promote clean-up to its own gated step at the end of Execution, with a checklist (accounts, files, persistence, scheduled jobs, config changes) and an explicit sign-off recorded in the engagement record.

title: Operator-driven threat-actor selection is not informed by a TTI product; acceptable here but should be stated so it cannot be cited as TIBER
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Define and document the profiles of potential threat actors that will be emulated during the red team operation … Research known threat actors relevant to your organization or industry to gain insights into their past activities and behaviours.' Threat actor selection is performed by the operators themselves; there is no reference to an independent Threat Intelligence (TI) provider, a Targeted Threat Intelligence (TTI) report, or a Generic Threat Landscape (GTL) input.
expected: For a Threat Scenario this operator-driven approach is acceptable and this finding is not asking for a TI provider. What is missing is the explicit positioning so the doc cannot be cited as evidence of TIBER-aligned threat intelligence. TIBER-EU's Threat Intelligence phase requires an independent TI provider producing a TTI report; this doc does not.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — Threat Intelligence phase: GTL consumption, TTI report by independent TI provider (Cited from memory.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26/27 (general) and the related RTS on TLPT — independent threat intelligence provider requirement (Cited from memory; verify against live RTS on TLPT.)
recommendation_text: Either (a) keep operator-driven threat-actor selection but note explicitly that this differs from TIBER-EU's TI phase, or (b) when a Threat Scenario is intended to feed broader TLPT preparation, document the consumption of an existing TTI/GTL produced for an Exercise so the inputs are traceable.
owner_hint: Red team lead with Threat intel

title: No jurisdictional / regulated-entity scope statement — Euronext spans multiple NCAs
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the document never references Euronext's regulated entities or the relevant National Competent Authorities (AFM, AMF, FSMA, CSSF, CONSOB and others) and does not state which entities the process applies to.
expected: A line stating which Euronext entities and jurisdictions this process applies to, so that any later use of Threat-Scenario outputs as DORA / TIBER-XX evidence is traceable to the right NCA's expectations.
framework_refs: OTHER:TIBER-XX national implementations (AFM, AMF, FSMA, CSSF, CONSOB, etc.) (User to map this process to the relevant national TIBER-XX implementation per regulated entity.); DORA:DORA (general) — applicability to financial entities and group structures (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.)
recommendation_text: Add a one-line scope-of-applicability statement: which Euronext regulated entities and jurisdictions the Threat Scenario process applies to. If multiple entities, note which NCA's TIBER-XX guidance is the reference point should the outputs be cited as resilience-testing evidence.

title: Kill-switch authority is asserted but not procedurally defined
evidence_quote_or_observation: Link between the RT and the BT, and consists of employees who can escalate and de-escalate with both teams. Informed of all attacks by the Red Team and is mandated to start, stop and approve attacks.
expected: A documented kill-switch / abort procedure: who can invoke, how (channel, safe-word), what RT must do on receipt (stop, snapshot, preserve), and how resumption is authorized.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Risk management of the test; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3.4 Logistics / handling unexpected events
recommendation_text: Add a Kill-Switch / Abort procedure: communication channel (out-of-band), safe-word, expected RT actions on abort (cease, preserve evidence, notify), and resumption protocol with WT sign-off.

title: Scoping phase is implicit; objectives are defined 'at the end of the 1st phase' with no scoping artefact
evidence_quote_or_observation: These objectives are defined at the end of the 1st Phase as explained in the following sections, and during the RT Test on the 2nd Phase, anything is in scope to achieve these objectives.
expected: A discrete Scoping step with a scoping document listing in-scope systems, out-of-scope systems, objectives, success criteria, and constraints, signed off before execution. 'Anything is in scope' is too broad for an Operation.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement / Scope; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Preparation / scoping; MITRE-ATTACK:MITRE ATT&CK tactics (Initial Access, Lateral Movement, Impact); OTHER:Unified Kill Chain (Pols, 2017)
recommendation_text: Introduce a named Scoping step with a scoping document template (in-scope, out-of-scope, objectives, success criteria, constraints, third-party dependencies) and require WT sign-off before the RT Test begins. Replace 'anything is in scope' with explicit boundaries.

title: Leg-up authorization criteria and audit trail not defined
evidence_quote_or_observation: During the assessment, the RT may be unable to progress to the next stage (due to time constraints, business knowledge constraints or because the entity has been successful in protecting itself), RT with agreement from the WT, may be given access to its system, internal network, etc. to continue with the test and focus on the next objective/target.
expected: Leg-up should be governed by a written request/approval, recorded in OPLOGs and the final report (so that 'success' is not later misread when a leg-up was granted), with criteria stating when a leg-up is appropriate vs when the scenario should be marked unconcluded.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Red Team Test / leg-ups; PTES:PTES Exploitation / pre-engagement constraints
recommendation_text: Add a Leg-Up procedure: written request from RT to WT, criteria for approval, mandatory recording in OPLOG and final report (including which objective was reached only with a leg-up), and rules for when a scenario should instead be marked 'not concluded'.

title: Threat intelligence sourcing does not distinguish open-source feeds from a TIBER-EU Targeted Threat Intelligence (TTI) report
evidence_quote_or_observation: These can come from external sources, such as blog posts from research companies (ex: SpecterOps, BishopFox, Bad Sector Labs, ...) describing new EDR evasion techniques, new phishing techniques, new delivery methods or new AD vulnerabilities. These opportunities can also come from the CTI team, when new CVEs affecting company assets are published with Proof of Concept (PoC) code, or if a Threat Report (ex: Crowdstrike Threat Report 2024) gets published that contains technical details of TTPs currently deployed by relevant Threat Actors.
expected: For internal Operations, opportunistic / open-source TI is acceptable. For TIBER-EU / DORA TLPT, an independent Threat Intelligence provider must produce a Targeted Threat Intelligence (TTI) report that drives scenarios. The SOP should distinguish the two so that an Operation is not later miscategorised as a TLPT.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Threat Intelligence phase / TTI report (Cited from memory.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 / RTS on TLPT - threat intelligence provider independence (Cited from memory; verify against live text.)
recommendation_text: Add a paragraph clarifying that Operations rely on opportunistic / internal CTI inputs and that any engagement intended to satisfy TLPT must additionally procure an independent TI provider producing a TTI report. Verify the TI-provider independence clause against the live RTS on TLPT.

title: Critical / important functions (DORA terminology) are not used to drive scope
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> (the document does not reference 'critical functions', 'important business services', or any DORA scope concept; objectives are defined per-Operation without anchoring to a critical-functions register)
expected: Operations targeting Euronext production estate should anchor scope/objectives to the institution's register of critical or important functions, so that Operation evidence can later support DORA operational-resilience-testing reporting.
framework_refs: DORA:DORA Art.8 (general) - identification of critical or important functions (Cited from memory; verify article number.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 - TLPT scope based on critical functions (Cited from memory.)
recommendation_text: Add to Scoping a requirement that each Operation's objectives reference one or more critical or important functions from the institution's register, so that Operations evidence is reusable for DORA reporting. Verify the precise DORA article references against the live regulation.

title: No lessons-learned capture mechanism into the team knowledge base
evidence_quote_or_observation: WT responsibilities mention 'Providing list of lessons learned through observation during and after action' and the Deconfliction section mentions 'after-action reviews', but there is no concrete lessons-learned artefact, owner, repository, or capture template tied to RT process improvement.
expected: A defined lessons-learned step in Closure with a template, an owner (RT lead), and a target location (team wiki / knowledge base) so improvements feed back into future exercise planning.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Post-Engagement (Lessons Learned)
recommendation_text: Add a Closure step 'Lessons learned': structured retro covering RT, WT and (where appropriate) BT input, captured in the team knowledge base using a fixed template; include explicit follow-up actions and owners.

title: TI provider independence not addressed; TI appears to be performed by the same team as RT
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Threat Intelligence Team (TI) - Responsible for performing Threat Actors investigation...' The TI Team is described as a sub-team but the document does not state whether TI is independent of RT, nor what happens when an external TI provider is engaged. The 'During this phase, the RT team focuses on gathering information' wording implies overlap.
expected: Clarify whether TI is performed by an independent team / external provider; if TI and RT are the same people, document the rationale and the risk to objectivity. Required for any future TLPT framing.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Intelligence Gathering; TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Threat Intelligence phase (independent TI provider) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.)
recommendation_text: Add a paragraph in Targeted TI stating who can perform the TI (internal TI function, external provider) and confirming separation from the RT executors when feasible. Note that TLPT framing requires an independent TI provider.
owner_hint: Threat intel lead + Red team lead

title: Stealth / OPSEC posture and detection-as-result not explicitly captured as a phase outcome
evidence_quote_or_observation: OPSEC is defined in Terms ('used by Red Team Operators to determine how likely their actions are to be caught') but the RT Test phase does not state that stealth is in scope, that BT detections must be logged with timestamps, or that detection events themselves are a primary result of the exercise. 'In all steps of this phase, the operators should keep in mind the scope and restrictions defined' is the closest.
expected: RT Test should state that stealth is in scope, that any blue-team detection must be timestamped and recorded against the corresponding TTP, and that 'time to detect' / 'time to respond' is a deliverable of the exercise.
framework_refs: MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK Detection coverage (per technique); PTES:PTES Vulnerability Analysis / Exploitation (OPSEC)
recommendation_text: Add an 'OPSEC and detection capture' note to the RT Test phase: stealth is in scope; the RT records every BT detection event (or absence thereof) per TTP; produce time-to-detect / time-to-contain metrics as a Closure deliverable.

title: Diagrams referenced but not ingested / not described in prose
evidence_quote_or_observation: The doc references Figure 1 (Red Teaming Phases), Figure 2 (Teams Involvement in each Phase), Figure 3 (Expected activities' timeline), Figure 4 (Deconfliction Process), Figure 5 (Unified Kill Chain), Figure 6 (Example OPLOG entry). None are present as captions or alt-text in content.md and no diagram.json was produced. A reviewer cannot verify swimlane / phase / timing claims.
expected: Each figure should be described in prose (or a caption) so the document is self-contained, and the diagrams should be ingested separately with diagram.json/captions.md so they can be reviewed against the prose.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Reporting (clarity / evidence)
recommendation_text: Either re-ingest the document with diagram extraction enabled, or add prose descriptions / captions next to each figure reference (Phase ownership matrix, timeline, deconfliction flow, kill chain). Critical for the diagram-consistency evaluator to be meaningful.

title: Reporting deliverable lacks defined finding taxonomy and severity model
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'In this report, there should also be information regarding the vulnerabilities found, similar to a regular Pentesting report, with severity, description, impact and meaningful recommendations'. No defined severity model (e.g. CVSS / business-impact rubric), no finding taxonomy, no required evidence per finding (screenshots, OPLOG references).
expected: Closure should specify: (a) the severity model used, (b) the required evidence per finding (OPLOG IDs, screenshots, artefacts), (c) link from each finding to MITRE ATT&CK technique IDs, (d) link from each finding to the scenario it supports.
framework_refs: MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK technique mapping per finding; PTES:PTES Reporting
recommendation_text: Define the reporting taxonomy: severity rubric, mandatory evidence fields, ATT&CK technique ID per finding, and scenario-to-finding linkage. Provide a report template.

title: Leg-Up process lacks logging requirements and impact on results / detection metrics
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'In such scenarios, the RT with agreement from the WT, may be given a leg-up: RT is given access to its system, internal network, etc. to continue with the test and focus on the next flag/target.' The doc does not require: (a) the leg-up to be logged with rationale, (b) it to be flagged in the report so the reader knows which findings rely on a leg-up, (c) the impact on detection-time metrics to be acknowledged.
expected: Leg-Ups should be logged (date, what was provided, rationale, requesting RT operator, approving WT member); each finding produced after a leg-up must be tagged as 'leg-up assisted' in the report.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 leg-up handling (Closure transparency) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.); PTES:PTES Reporting (transparency); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 RTT (transparency on leg-ups in closure) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.)
recommendation_text: Update Leg-Ups section to require: (1) entry in an engagement leg-up log, (2) WT countersignature, (3) explicit tagging of any downstream finding as 'leg-up assisted' in the Closure report.
owner_hint: Red team lead + White team lead

title: Data-protection clauses do not anchor on GDPR / DORA / sector regulation
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'During the engagement, the operator will not intentionally compromise Privacy of Information Act (PIA), medical, justice, worship or religious pursuit, or any other protected or privileged information' — references a non-EU statute and lists categories that don't map to GDPR special categories or to MiFID/MAR-relevant categories (market-sensitive, inside information).
expected: Anchor data-handling on the EU regimes that actually apply: GDPR (with attention to special categories under Art.9), MAR (inside information), MiFID II (client data), and the DORA confidentiality requirements applicable during TLPT testing. State the legal basis under GDPR for processing personal data encountered during the test.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA / RTS on TLPT (confidentiality of test artefacts) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation and RTS text.); OTHER:GDPR Art.6 (lawful basis), Art.9 (special categories) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.)
recommendation_text: Rewrite Data Handling around: GDPR (lawful basis, special categories), MAR/MiFID inside-information and client-data confidentiality, DORA TLPT confidentiality requirements. Provide a decision tree: encountered-data type -> required action (halt, notify, delete, retain under controls).

title: Scenarios derived from TI but no Targeted Threat Intelligence Report deliverable named
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Scenarios Description... Each scenario should include a description, the threat actors related to the scenario...' and TI Team responsibility 'Performs Threat Intelligence Report with Threat Landscape of the Company and OSINT investigation'. There is mention of a TI report but no formal Targeted Threat Intelligence (TTI) Report artefact tied to the TIBER-EU TI phase, with named contents and approver.
expected: Name a Targeted Threat Intelligence Report artefact with required contents (threat-actor profiles, TTPs, attack hypotheses, mapping to critical functions), an approver (CT/WT) and a version under document control.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Threat Intelligence phase (TTI Report) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.)
recommendation_text: Define a 'Targeted Threat Intelligence Report' deliverable in the TI phase, with template (threat actors, TTPs, attack hypotheses, scenario seeds, ATT&CK mapping), a named approver, and version control.

title: Use of 'Unified Kill Chain' is fine but no mapping to TIBER-EU 'flags / legs / objectives' construct
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'During the execution, the RT Engagement attacks will be modelled after he Unified Kill Chain framework that it's Attack Phases can be found summarized in Figure 5.' Unified Kill Chain is reasonable, but TIBER-EU and most TLPT testing organize the RT Test around 'flags' (intermediate objectives), 'legs' (paths), and final objectives, which is the language regulators expect to see in TLPT closure documentation.
expected: Map the Unified Kill Chain phases to TIBER-EU 'legs / flags / objectives' so the same RT Test can be reported in either vocabulary; for TLPT framing, the closure report should use flags/objectives terminology.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Red Team Test phase (legs, flags, objectives) (Cited from memory; verify against live framework text.); MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK technique mapping per TTP
recommendation_text: Add a paragraph in RT Test mapping Unified Kill Chain phases to TIBER-EU 'legs / flags / objectives'. Require ATT&CK technique IDs at each step. Provide a sample mapping table.

title: Document refers to 'a company' generically; jurisdictional applicability under DORA is not stated
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'This document describes the Red Team Process that the Red Team should adhere to when planning and executing a Red Team Exercise against a company'. Euronext is a multi-entity, multi-jurisdiction group; DORA TLPT applicability differs by entity (e.g. Euronext NV, Euronext Amsterdam, Brussels, Paris, Lisbon, Milan, Dublin, Oslo). The doc does not clarify which entities are in scope or how the SOP varies by entity.
expected: Add an applicability matrix: which Euronext entities the SOP covers, which are likely DORA TLPT subjects (and under which lead overseer), and how the SOP differs (e.g. TLPT addendum) for each.
framework_refs: OTHER:ISO/IEC 27001:2022 A.5.1 (Policies for information security) (Cited from memory; document-control good practice.); DORA:DORA scope (financial entities, significant financial entities) (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.); OTHER:TIBER-XX national implementations (AFM, AMF, FSMA, CSSF, CONSOB, CMVM)
recommendation_text: Add an entity / jurisdiction applicability section: list in-scope Euronext entities, identify likely lead overseers, and link to per-entity TLPT addenda or NCA-specific guidance.

title: Service-account privilege model is described but not safety-bounded (e.g. optional Local Admin everywhere)
evidence_quote_or_observation: '[OPTIONAL] Local Administrator on all domain-joined systems' and 'LDAP will attempt LDAP over SSL first, then fall back to LDAP if SSL is unavailable.'
expected: Explicit guidance that the optional Local Admin grant is generally NOT taken at Euronext (or, if taken, with a documented justification), plus a stance on the LDAP-cleartext fallback (block fallback or accept residual risk and log).
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:§4 — handling of privileged test accounts; MITRE-ATTACK:T1003/T1078 — risks of broad-privilege service accounts
recommendation_text: State Euronext's stance on the optional Local-Admin-everywhere grant (default: NO) and on LDAP cleartext fallback (default: blocked); record exceptions with a named approver and review date.

title: Cleanup / rollback responsibility is described informally and lacks a verification gate
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'After exploitation is completed and the vulnerability is fixed, the changes done during exploitation should be cleaned-up. ... To simplify this process, the team member performing the exploitation should keep detailed logs of their activity and use the OPLOGS recommended in the Red Team Exercises process.'
expected: A second-pair-of-eyes verification step: after self-cleanup, a peer reviews the OPLOG and confirms each modification was reverted, signed off in the engagement record.
framework_refs: PTES:Post-Exploitation — Cleanup; NIST-800-115:§5.6 — Post-test cleanup
recommendation_text: Require peer-reviewed cleanup verification: a second team member checks the OPLOG against environment state and signs off; record the sign-off in the engagement file.

title: User-account-impact rule is sound but lacks a defined approver and audit trail
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'no action should be taken on a User account without the user’s acknowledgement and written permission.'
expected: Define what 'written permission' means (signed form, ticket, email-with-approver), where it is filed, and who else (manager, IAM) must counter-sign for service-account or privileged-account actions.
framework_refs: PTES:Pre-engagement / Authorization — written authorization; NIST-800-115:§3.4 — Rules of Engagement; PTES:Vulnerability Analysis / Exploitation — operational security
recommendation_text: Specify the format and storage of the user written permission (e.g. ticket with manager approval), require IAM counter-signature for service or privileged accounts, and reference the engagement file where the artefact is stored.

title: Doc covers on-prem AD but only nods at Entra ID; cloud-identity attack paths under-treated
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'BloodHound uses graph theory to reveal the hidden and often unintended relationships within Active Directory and Entra ID.' Beyond the introduction, all collection, Tier-0 and analysis examples are AD-only; Entra ID specifics (collection prerequisites, cloud Tier-0, hybrid attack paths) are absent.
expected: An Entra ID section: collection prerequisites and permissions, cloud Tier-0 examples (Global Admin / Application Admin / PIM-eligible roles), hybrid attack paths (AAD Connect, federation), and a stance on whether cloud collection is enabled at Euronext.
framework_refs: MITRE-ATTACK:Enterprise — Cloud / Identity techniques (T1078.004, T1556.007, T1098.005); OSSTMM:Channels — Human/Physical (out of scope here)
recommendation_text: Add an 'Entra ID' subsection covering collection setup, cloud Tier-0 baseline, hybrid attack paths, and a current-state note on whether cloud collection is enabled at Euronext.

title: Sensitive AD/Entra graph data is uploaded to a SaaS tenant without DORA-aligned ICT third-party / data-handling controls in the document
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'This information is then uploaded to Euronext tenant to be analysed.' / 'SAML is not yet implemented, and only application accounts are used.' The doc does not address ICT third-party risk management of the BHE SaaS provider, data residency, contractual exit, or sub-outsourcing — the BHE tenant holds the full attack-path graph of the directory.
expected: Even though this is a tooling doc, it should reference the corporate ICT third-party register entry for the BHE SaaS, data-residency, exit clause and sub-outsourcing — DORA explicitly governs ICT third-party providers supporting critical or important functions (and red team support to a TLPT may qualify).
framework_refs: DORA:Art. 28–30 (general) — ICT third-party risk management and contractual provisions (Verify exact article numbering against the live Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 text.); DORA-TLPT:RTS on TLPT (general) — handling of test data / evidence
recommendation_text: Reference the ICT third-party register entry for the BHE SaaS in this document (or in a linked Tooling Inventory), and ensure data-residency, exit-clause and sub-outsourcing items are tracked there. Verify the precise DORA articles cited against the live text.

title: Three engagement types named but Purple Team is presented inconsistently as a fourth type
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'For the Red Team, there are three types of engagements: - Red Team Threat Scenarios - Red Team Operations - Red Team Exercises - Purple Team'. The list is introduced as 'three types' but enumerates four bullets.
expected: The methodology should clearly state that there are three engagement types (Threat Scenario, Operation, Exercise) and frame Purple Teaming as the mandatory close-out activity that follows each, not as a fourth type.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions (Engagement taxonomy must be unambiguous so scoping conversations are reliable.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) — TLPT is for advanced testing of ICT processes (Memory cite — verify.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general)
recommendation_text: Rewrite the introduction to list the three engagement types and describe Purple Teaming separately as a mandatory close-out across all three. Align with the team's published engagement taxonomy.

title: Exercise type described as 'TIBER-Like' without committing to TIBER-EU phase model or actor model
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Red Team Exercises, are full Red Team engagements, well-planned and TIBER-Like. They are design to mimic a specific Threat Actor TTPs…'
expected: The methodology should either (a) commit to running Exercises as TIBER-EU and then map to its phases (Preparation, TI, RTT, Closure) and actors (TCT, CT, WT, TI provider, RT provider), or (b) make explicit that 'TIBER-Like' is inspiration only, not regulatory alignment.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — phase model and actor model; DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) — TLPT scope
recommendation_text: Replace 'TIBER-Like' with one of two precise statements: (i) Exercises are run as TIBER-EU / DORA TLPT engagements and follow Preparation → TI → RTT → Closure with TCT/CT/WT/TI/RT actors; or (ii) Exercises are inspired by TIBER-EU but are not TLPT and do not satisfy DORA Art.26 — adjust the rest of the doc accordingly.

title: Safety / blast-radius controls limited to 'Forbidden Attacks' list; no testing-in-prod safeguards or rollback model
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Generally speaking, any attacks that can cause a Denial of Service of any kind are strictly forbidden… To avoid unintended Denial of Service attacks the operators should test their exploits in a controlled lab environment before using them in live systems.'
expected: Beyond a forbidden-attacks list, the methodology should describe blast-radius controls (lab→staging→prod gating, change windows, rollback steps for persistence implants/registry keys, communication protocol with WT/CT before high-impact actions).
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — RTT risk management; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 Planning — risk to test environment
recommendation_text: Expand the Forbidden Attacks section into a 'Safety & blast-radius controls' section: lab-first testing requirement (already present), explicit rollback/cleanup expectations for persistence and registry artefacts, change-window communication with WT, and pre-action notification triggers for high-impact actions.

title: Data Handling references PIA (US act) and 'WT Point of Contact' but never defines WT or applies GDPR / EU data protection
evidence_quote_or_observation: '…will not intentionally compromise Privacy of Information Act (PIA), medical, justice, worship or religious pursuit, or any other protected or privileged information…' and 'WT Point of Contact should be promptly contacted to confirm the type of data of the files'.
expected: Data Handling rules for an EU regulated institution must reference GDPR (and DORA data-handling expectations) as the applicable legal basis, not US 'PIA'. The White Team / Control Team should be defined elsewhere in the methodology, not introduced only as 'WT Point of Contact'.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — Control Team / White Team; DORA:DORA — data protection during testing (general) (Memory cite — verify.)
recommendation_text: Replace 'PIA' with the relevant EU legal basis (GDPR Art.6/9, applicable national law) and add explicit personal-data and confidential-data categories. Define the White Team / Control Team in a dedicated section so the 'WT Point of Contact' reference is anchored.

title: Threat Scenario description omits internal-tool abuse scope (GitLab/Jenkins/CI/CD/secret stores) and joint-planning structure
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Red Team Threat Scenarios, also known as Objective Oriented Pentest, are penetration tests done on specific technologies with the completion of pre-defined scenarios in mind, not just trying to find as many vulnerabilities as possible…'
expected: The methodology should describe Threat Scenarios as assumed-breach demonstrative engagements with the blue team in the room, focused on internal-tool abuse paths (GitLab, Jenkins, CI/CD, internal SaaS, ticketing, secret stores), with joint planning and real-time control discussion. 'Objective Oriented Pentest' framing under-specifies this.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Vulnerability Analysis / Exploitation; MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK — abuse of legitimate services
recommendation_text: Update the Threat Scenario paragraph to describe the assumed-breach, demonstrative, joint-planning structure and call out the typical internal-tool targets (GitLab, Jenkins, CI/CD, internal SaaS, secret stores, ticketing). State explicitly that stealth is not in scope.

title: Operation defined as 'opportunistic, outside formalized engagement' — inconsistent with assumed-breach model; weakens authorization
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Red Team Operations, are opportunistic type of attacks that are conducted outside the context of a formalized engagement. These type of engagements are used to test hypotheses, prove risks…'
expected: Operations should be defined as assumed-breach, scenario-bound engagements with a small set of pre-defined objectives, formal authorization, and a codename — not as informal opportunistic activity 'outside the context of a formalized engagement', which implies relaxed authorization.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions; NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 Planning
recommendation_text: Rewrite the Operations paragraph: 'Operations are assumed-breach, scenario-scoped engagements with formal authorization, codename, and a small set of pre-defined objectives. They are typically used to test specific hypotheses or prove risks, and follow the Operations Process document.' Drop the 'outside the context of a formalized engagement' wording.

title: Jurisdictional ambiguity: methodology silent on which Euronext regulated entity / NCA the framework applies under
evidence_quote_or_observation: <missing> — the methodology does not name a lead overseer or competent authority and does not address the multi-jurisdictional nature of Euronext (e.g. AFM, AMF, CMVM, FSMA, CONSOB) for TIBER-XX implementations.
expected: Given Euronext operates regulated venues across multiple EU jurisdictions, the methodology should at minimum acknowledge that the applicable NCA / TIBER-XX guidance must be identified per engagement, and reference the appropriate per-NCA procedure.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) — NCA-specific TIBER-XX implementations; DORA-TLPT:DORA — competent authority / lead overseer (general) (Memory cite — verify.); OTHER:TIBER-NL / TIBER-FR / TIBER-BE / TIBER-PT / TIBER-IT national guidance
recommendation_text: Add a short jurisdictional clause stating that for any engagement positioned as TIBER-EU / TLPT, the applicable NCA and the corresponding TIBER-XX implementation must be identified per engagement, with the lead overseer recorded in the engagement letter.

title: AI-enrichment layer described without governance, prompt provenance, or data-handling rules
evidence_quote_or_observation: "All research inputs pass through a the AI Enrichment layer that generates: Research summary, MITRE TTPs mapping, Research categories, Priority (environment-aware), Affected technologies" and "The process follows an AI-assisted, human-validated model". The doc does not specify which model/service is used, whether prompts/inputs may include internal context, retention, or auditability of AI outputs.
expected: Stated AI provider/model class, data-handling rules (no internal sensitive data sent unless on an approved tenant), prompt/version control, and audit trail of AI outputs that influenced prioritisation.
framework_refs: OTHER:ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems) / NIST AI RMF (Reference frameworks for governance of AI components inside a security workflow.)
recommendation_text: Add an 'AI Enrichment governance' paragraph covering approved model/tenant, data-classification rules for inputs, prompt and version control, and how AI outputs are logged for audit.

title: Purple-team feedback loop named but not defined as a gated close-out
evidence_quote_or_observation: "Finally, the RT Engagement should follow the process established with the Purple Team to identify Detections and Controls gaps." Phrased as a should and delegated to another (unnamed) process; no requirement that purple-team output drive new research entries or capability deprecation.
expected: Mandatory, gated purple-team close-out that produces structured input back into the Research Repository and Tool Repository (e.g. detection notes, deprecations, hunt hypotheses).
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 Closure - 360 deg replay / purple-teaming (Cited as the regulator-recognised purple-team close-out shape.)
recommendation_text: Make purple-team close-out mandatory and define the structured artefacts it must produce that feed both the Research and Tool repositories (detection deltas, capability lifecycle changes, hunt hypotheses).

title: Physical / social-engineering tradecraft research not explicitly in or out of scope
evidence_quote_or_observation: In Scope lists only "External offensive security research (blogs, whitepapers, ...)" and three other cyber-flavoured items; Out of Scope lists no physical exclusion. The doc is silent on physical-intrusion or social-engineering tradecraft research even though Euronext red team scope includes physical components.
expected: Explicit statement that physical and social-engineering tradecraft research is in scope (or, with rationale, out of scope) so the methodology matches the team's actual remit.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM Channel Coverage - Human + Physical channels (OSSTMM treats human and physical as first-class test channels.)
recommendation_text: Add an explicit line clarifying scope of physical / social-engineering tradecraft research; align with the physical-social process docs.

title: OPSEC and detection notes mandated but no separation between research-time and engagement-time disclosure
evidence_quote_or_observation: "OPSEC and detection notes" are required on Tool Repository entries, and Deep Dive must capture "OPSEC considerations". The doc does not differentiate between OPSEC notes useful internally and OPSEC notes that, if leaked, could compromise live engagements - i.e. no marking / handling tier.
expected: A handling tier on OPSEC and detection notes (e.g. internal-only, engagement-active, releasable to purple) so that publication / handover does not leak active tradecraft.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM Operational Security - information classification
recommendation_text: Introduce a small handling tier on OPSEC / detection notes (e.g. RT-only, share-with-purple, releasable) so capability documentation is shared safely.

title: Research provenance not bound to engagement deliverables - traceability risk for TLPT attestation
evidence_quote_or_observation: "a central source of truth for research outputs that supports RT Exercises, Operations, Threat Scenarios, and Purple Teaming." and "with clear documentation and usage guidance, linked research references". The doc does not require Research Repository entry IDs or Tool Repository entry IDs to be cited from engagement scenario / RTT / closure deliverables.
expected: Each capability used in a TIBER-EU Red Team Test or DORA TLPT engagement should be traceable from the closure / attestation report back to a Research Repository entry and Tool Repository lifecycle state, supporting an auditable chain of custody for tradecraft.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) - Closure phase, replay and attestation (Cited from memory - verify against the live ECB TIBER-EU v2 text.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) - test summary report and attestation (Cited from memory - verify against Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 and the TLPT RTS.)
recommendation_text: Require engagement deliverables (RTT report, closure report, attestation pack) to cite the Research Repository and Tool Repository IDs of the techniques and capabilities used, so research provenance is auditable for TLPT.

title: Reproduction of techniques against prod-like environments needs DORA test-risk-management framing
evidence_quote_or_observation: "The assigned operator must reproduce and validate the techniques, perform environment-specific testing and analysis of detection and control impacts." 'Environment-specific' is undefined; if reproduction touches systems shared with TLPT-eligible critical or important functions, the test-risk-management requirements (safe harbours, kill-switch, data protection) of DORA TLPT apply.
expected: Explicit statement that any reproduction touching environments shared with critical or important functions (as scoped under DORA TLPT) inherits TLPT test-risk-management controls: safe harbour, kill-switch, data-protection, and authorization by the control team / control function.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) - risk management of the test, safety controls (Cited from memory - verify against Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 and the TLPT RTS.); TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 (general) - Red Team Test phase, controlled escalation and safe harbours (Cited from memory - verify against the live ECB TIBER-EU v2 text.)
recommendation_text: Add a paragraph that escalates a reproduction to TLPT test-risk-management controls when the environment touched is shared with critical or important functions; require control-team authorisation and reference DORA Art.26 safety controls.

section_ref: Physical Pentest / Planning / Get out of jail letter
title: Get-out-of-jail letter does not explicitly cover indemnification, insurance, bail funding or counsel access
evidence_quote_or_observation: "With this objectives in mind, the letter should clearly state the following information: Name of Authorizer / Full address of the Office Premises / Nature of Testing Activities / Testing Period / Authorized Team Members (Full Name and National Citizen Card ID) / Point of Contact Information (Full Name, Position, Contact Information) / Signed by Authorizer". No mention of company indemnification, third-party liability insurance, bail funding, access to counsel if detained, or coverage if criminal charges are pursued.
expected: Letter and surrounding policy explicitly state company indemnification of operators acting within scope, insurance coverage for property damage / personal injury, bail-and-counsel arrangements if operators are detained, and the legal route if charges are pursued.
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3.3 Legal Considerations; OSSTMM:OSSTMM PHYSSEC Rules of Engagement
recommendation_text: Update the Get-out-of-jail letter template and policy to reference an indemnification clause, evidence of liability insurance, a 24/7 legal contact for detained operators, and bail/counsel funding commitments.

title: Blue-team notification posture is undefined - secrecy clause names only Local CISO and Local Facilities Manager
evidence_quote_or_observation: "it is import that the operators emphasize the need to keep the existence of the exercise only to the Local CISO and Local Facilities Manager." The document does not state whether SOC, Group CISO, Group Security or the blue team is notified, or kept in the dark for stealth purposes.
expected: A clear blue-team-notification rule per assessment type: Pentest stealth (SOC blind), Audit (announced), Questionnaire (announced), with the named recipients and timing of any post-engagement disclosure.
framework_refs: TIBER-EU:TIBER-EU v2 White Team / Control Team awareness; PTES:PTES Pre-engagement Interactions
recommendation_text: Add a 'Notification matrix' to Planning specifying for each assessment type who is in the know (white team) and who is blind (blue team / SOC), and when and how disclosure happens after the test.

title: Weaponization step has no inventory, hand-back or implant-removal control
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This includes, but not limited to, having hardware implants to leave on the office location attached to ethernet ports, Rubber Duckies to leave around the office, card cloning technologies to test the access controls used by employees." There is no rule for inventorying implants taken in, accounting for which were left where, or guaranteed retrieval/decommissioning at engagement close.
expected: A weaponization inventory and chain-of-custody log, and a mandatory step at close-out that confirms every implant has been retrieved or, if abandoned, formally disclosed to the white team and Local Facilities Manager.
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6.2 Cleanup; OSSTMM:OSSTMM PHYSSEC instrumentation control
recommendation_text: Add a hardware-implant inventory, chain-of-custody log, and explicit 'recovery and decommissioning' close-out step.

title: No safe handover from physical foothold to cyber post-exploitation team
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This might include connecting a rogue device to exposed ethernet ports, dropping rubber duckies or installing any other type of implants at the location." No description of how an established physical foothold is handed over to the cyber red team operators (or vice-versa) within an Exercise / Operation, nor what authorization re-confirmation occurs at handover.
expected: A described handover from physical leg to cyber leg (and back), including who authorizes the foothold to be used, what segmentation / kill-switch applies, and how the network impact is bounded.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Post-Exploitation; MITRE-ATTACK:MITRE ATT&CK T1200 Hardware Additions
recommendation_text: Document the physical-to-cyber and cyber-to-physical handover including authorization re-confirmation, network segmentation/kill-switch and termination criteria.

title: Critical / important functions not used to scope physical testing
evidence_quote_or_observation: "As part of planning of physical assessments, the Red Team defines a criticality level for each location, and based on that criticality level, defines what type of assessment needs to be performed." Criticality is location-based (People / Network / Hardware) and not tied to the DORA notion of 'critical or important functions'.
expected: Where the test feeds DORA evidence, the location-criticality model is reconciled with the DORA 'critical or important functions' identified for the entity, so a location hosting a critical function is always a Pentest candidate regardless of headcount/network metrics.
framework_refs: DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26(2) - TLPT covers critical or important functions
recommendation_text: Reconcile the location-criticality model with the entity's DORA register of critical or important functions; add an override that any location hosting a critical function automatically requires Pentest-grade testing.

title: Introduction copy describes a different scope than the questionnaire actually covers
evidence_quote_or_observation: "This document describes the Red Team methodology that all Red Team members should adhere to when performing a Physical Assessments against a critical company tool, application or process."
expected: The introduction should match the questionnaire's actual purpose (a periodic physical-security assessment of office locations) rather than asserting it is a methodology for assessing 'a critical company tool, application or process'.
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §3 (Planning) — clarity of test scope and target (A scoping artefact should unambiguously describe its target environment.)
recommendation_text: Rewrite the Introduction to state that this is a periodic Physical Security Questionnaire issued to Euronext office locations (per CMDB criticality), referencing the parent PhysicalSocial process for the engagement-led testing flow.

title: Periodicity adjustment logic is described informally and not bound to the maturity score
evidence_quote_or_observation: "For offices marked as critical, the questionnaires will be sent, maximum, every twelve (12) months. For non-critical locations, they will be sent every thirty-six (36) months. The periodicity of questionnaires will be adjusted based on number of vulnerabilities open and missing controls." and later "The Maturity will then be used to calculate the frequency, in months, for that specific location based on the following number:" — the lookup table is missing.
expected: An explicit table mapping Maturity score bands to questionnaire periodicity (in months), with an upper bound for critical sites and a documented override for sites with active findings.
framework_refs: NIST-800-115:NIST SP 800-115 §6 (Post-Testing Activities) — risk-driven scheduling of follow-up assessments
recommendation_text: Add a Maturity-to-Periodicity table (e.g. Maturity ≥0.9 → 36 months, 0.7-0.9 → 24, 0.5-0.7 → 12, <0.5 → 6) and codify the override rule for sites with open critical findings.

title: Social-engineering and tailgating posture not surveyed
evidence_quote_or_observation: Despite the parent process being titled 'Physical & Social', the questionnaire surveys badges, doors, CCTV and shredders but does not ask about anti-tailgating controls, security-awareness training, phishing-vishing posture, visitor-escort enforcement, or evidence-handling for social engineering. <missing>
expected: Question blocks covering tailgating mitigations (mantraps, turnstile enforcement), visitor escort policy and enforcement, social-engineering training cadence, and reception-side handling of unsolicited callers/visitors.
framework_refs: OSSTMM:OSSTMM Human Security channel — social engineering posture; MITRE-ATTACK:ATT&CK T1200 (Hardware Additions), T1566 (Phishing) — physical/social adversary techniques
recommendation_text: Add a Social Engineering and Tailgating block covering visitor-escort enforcement, anti-tailgating measures, awareness training cadence, and reception protocol for unsolicited contacts.

title: CODENAME section does not state the convention or pattern to use
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'It should be registered alongside the corresponding Threat Scenarios, linking the codename with the scope and a brief explanation on why it was chosen … The codename should not reveal any information about the target or the techniques being used.'
expected: Per Euronext's engagement-types reference, a Threat Scenario must require a codename but the fictional-character theme is not mandatory; the doc should still state which convention (theme, length, format) Threat Scenarios use, even if it is 'free choice subject to non-revelation'.
framework_refs: PTES:PTES Pre-engagement — engagement identifiers
recommendation_text: Document the Threat-Scenario codename convention explicitly (theme or 'no theme', allowed character set, register location). Cross-reference the Exercises and Operations conventions so the difference is intentional, not accidental.

title: Threat-actor selection guidance focuses on insiders but does not exclude relevant external actors
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Since most targets and scope are internal, the main Threat Actors considered are malicious insiders that want to impact the company.' — followed by an insider-only example table (Unauthorized User / Regular Plague User / Privileged Plague User).
expected: Acknowledgement that for internet-exposed internal tools (e.g. SaaS-hosted GitLab, externally reachable CI/CD) external actors with stolen credentials, supply-chain attackers and contractors are also valid Threat-Scenario actors, with a one-line decision rule for when to include them.
framework_refs: MITRE-ATTACK:MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise — Initial Access tactic (Cited from memory.)
recommendation_text: Update the Threat Actors guidance to include external-actor archetypes (compromised contractor, stolen-credential external attacker, supply-chain compromise) as candidates whenever the in-scope tool is reachable from outside the corporate perimeter or via a third party.
owner_hint: Red team lead with Threat intel

title: LLM-assisted scenario generation is recommended without guardrails on confidentiality / hallucination
evidence_quote_or_observation: 'Additionally, operators can also take advantage of AI and LLMs to request it to provide an overview of the Tool and the main Threat Scenarios that could be executed by Threat Actors to impact the Tools functionality.' — added in revision 1.1 per the version table.
expected: A short note on which LLM tools are approved, what data may be shared with them (no codename, no internal-only tool names, no real account details), and a reminder that LLM output must be peer-reviewed before it enters Planning, given hallucination risk.

title: OPLOG storage and integrity controls are unspecified

title: Clean-up step is described as a final note rather than a tracked phase

title: Questionnaire could surface DORA 'critical or important function' status of each office
evidence_quote_or_observation: The questionnaire uses CMDB-derived 'critical' / 'non-critical' classification to drive periodicity but never asks whether the site hosts a DORA-defined critical or important function (trading, clearing, market-data, custody-related infrastructure). <missing>
expected: A site-context question linking each office to DORA's 'critical or important function' notion so that questionnaire output can be reused as part of the institution's ICT risk-management evidence base under DORA Art.5-8.
framework_refs: DORA:DORA Art.8 (general) — identification of critical or important functions (Cited from memory; verify against live regulation text.); DORA-TLPT:DORA Art.26 (general) — TLPT scope tied to critical or important functions
recommendation_text: Add a Site Context question identifying whether the office supports a DORA critical or important function, and use the answer (alongside CMDB criticality) to drive questionnaire periodicity and sign-off escalation. This also makes the artefact reusable as DORA Art.5 record-keeping evidence.
